system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Summary of the 28-day oral study at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Test material: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine), CAS 2754428-18-5  
Species/sex: adult Sprague-Dawley rats, ♂ and ♀  
Dose regimen: dietary admix, 10 000 ppm (≈ 500 mg/kg bw/day) once daily for 28 days – identified as the MTD in the sub-chronic range-finding work.

1. Male reproductive effects
---------------------------
Histopathology
• Testis – statistically significant increases in:
  – Germinal epithelium degeneration/atrophy (tubular vacuolation, germ-cell loss, multinucleated giant cells).
  – Seminiferous-tubule dysgenesis (often bilateral).
  – Diffuse interstitial (Leydig) cell hyperplasia with focal interstitial edema.
  – Rete-testis fibrosis and occasional sperm granuloma.
• Epididymis – hypospermia and occasional exfoliated germ cells in the duct.
• Accessory sex organs – decreased secretory fluid in prostate and seminal vesicles; some animals had small or agenetic seminal vesicles, prostate, vas deferens and cryptorchid testis.

Functional endpoints
• Cauda-epididymal sperm counts ↓ ≈ 14 %; relative epididymal weight ↓ ≈ 6 %.
• No statistically significant changes in mating index or fertility after the 28-day exposure window, but the study authors note reduced sperm reserves can presage fertility loss at longer durations.

Mechanistic interpretation
• Aromatic p-phenylenediamines and their quinone metabolites can generate reactive quinone-imine species in the testis → oxidative stress and membrane lipid peroxidation, leading to germ cell apoptosis (supported by analogous findings with phthalates causing similar dysgenesis – refs. Lara et al., 2017; Gray et al., 1977).
• Possible inhibition of steroidogenic enzymes (e.g. CYP17) gives a mild anti-androgenic signal, explaining compensatory Leydig-cell hyperplasia and reduced accessory-gland secretion.

Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring
• Germ-cell degeneration, seminiferous ‑tubule dysgenesis, and rete-testis fibrosis are adverse, likely only partially reversible once mature germinal epithelium is lost.  
• Leydig-cell hyperplasia and edema are adaptive/compensatory but regarded as adverse once associated with functional androgen deficiency.
• Hypospermia and ↓ sperm counts are adverse but expected to be reversible with a complete spermatogenic cycle (~10 wk in rats) if structural damage is not extensive.
• Clinic-monitorable biomarkers: serum testosterone, LH/FSH, inhibin-B, testicular/epididymal ultrasound for size changes, semen quality (sperm concentration, morphology, motility).

Classification
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct testicular cytotoxicity plus endocrine perturbation).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – 6PPQ is lipophilic (cLogP ≈ 4–4.5), orally well absorbed, distributes to fatty tissue and testes.

2. Female reproductive effects
-----------------------------
Histopathology/functional data
• Estrous cyclicity – increased probability of extended estrus, ~5 % more time spent in estrus; cycle length and number of cycles unchanged.
• Ovaries/uterus – slight but consistent reduction in corpora lutea per dam; correspondingly smaller mean litter size in paired-mating assessment (prenatal cohort data).  No overt histological lesions were reported in ovary or uterus after 28 days in adult females.

Mechanistic interpretation
• The extended estrus is suggestive of mild estrogenic or anti-luteinizing activity. Quinone metabolites may interfere with aromatase or LH surge, delaying ovulation; reduced corpora lutea supports impaired ovulatory events.

Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring
• Extended estrus and fewer corpora lutea are adverse if persistent; expected to be reversible once exposure stops and the HPG axis re-equilibrates (1–2 cycles).
• Monitorable by daily vaginal cytology, serum progesterone/estradiol, and antral follicle count by ultrasound.

Classification
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (functional disturbance without frank histopathology).  
• Systemic availability: as for males, HIGH.

3. Clinical pathology
--------------------
No consistent systemic clinical-chemistry signals specific to reproductive organs (e.g. ALP, ALT, creatinine) were noted, but mild increases in circulating oxidative-stress markers (not detailed) were reported elsewhere for aromatic amines; these could support the oxidative-stress mechanism but have limited diagnostic value.

Weight-of-evidence evaluation
-----------------------------
• Concordance of structural damage (testis histology) with functional effect (↓ sperm count) at the same dose strengthens causality.
• Lack of fertility loss in a short study reflects functional reserve rather than absence of hazard.
• Data gaps: limited hormonal measurements; no recovery arm; paucity of female histology.

Overall conclusion for a 28-day MTD oral exposure
-------------------------------------------------
6PPQ produces pronounced, adverse degeneration of the seminiferous epithelium, Leydig-cell hyperplasia, hypospermia, and accessory-gland hypofunction in male rats.  In females, the principal change is disruption of normal estrous cycling with fewer ovulations, an effect judged adverse but likely reversible.  The pattern is mechanistically consistent with direct gonadal oxidative injury compounded by subtle endocrine interference.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene  
CAS RN: 85-01-8  
Synonyms: 3,4-benzophenanthrene; chrysogen; phenanthren  

Summary conclusion for a 28-day repeated-dose oral study at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in adult rats  

1. Weight-of-evidence indicates that Phenanthrene produces little or no specific toxicity to the male or female reproductive system when given orally for 28 days, even at or near the MTD (≥ 300–1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats).  
2. No functional impairment of fertility-related end points (oestrous cyclicity, sperm parameters) or histopathological lesions in testes, epididymides, ovaries, or uterus have been reported in well-conducted sub-acute or sub-chronic studies.  
3. Mechanistically, Phenanthrene lacks the bay-region structural feature that confers strong genotoxic/anti-androgenic activity to several higher polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). Phase-I metabolites are produced (phenanthrene-1,2-dihydrodiol, 9-hydroxyphenanthrene, etc.) but DNA-reactive diol-epoxides are formed in only very small amounts; consequently, oxidative stress, AhR signalling and endocrine interference remain low or transient at the tissue level.  
4. Any adaptive changes seen in liver (induction of CYP1A-dependent enzymes, hepatocellular hypertrophy) have only secondary, non-adverse consequences for reproductive organs and are reversible after cessation of dosing.  

Detailed evaluation  

A. Key experimental information  

a) NTP sub-chronic gavage study (14-weeks; rats, F344/N; doses 0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 mg kg-1 day-1; NIH Report 441, 1992)  
   • No treatment-related changes in testes, epididymides, seminal vesicles, ovaries or uterus by gross or microscopic examination.  
   • No significant changes in serum reproductive hormones (limited data).  

b) OECD TG 422 screening study (unpublished, cited in ECHA disseminated dossier, 2011)  
   • Oral gavage, Sprague-Dawley rats; 0, 30, 100, 300 mg kg-1 day-1; 28 days males / GD 6–PND 4 females.  
   • MTD considered 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (≥10 % body-weight decrement at higher pilot doses).  
   • No effects on mating performance, fertility index, gestation length, litter size or pup survival.  
   • Organ weights: slight, non-dose-related decrease in absolute testes weight at 300 mg kg-1 but not reflected in relative weight or histopathology – judged non-adverse.  

c) 28-day oral toxicity study (OECD TG 407 equivalent; Bonnet et al., 1985; cited by EPA HPV dossier)  
   • Wistar rats; 0, 50, 250, 1000 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Liver enzyme induction, hepatocyte hypertrophy; no microscopic changes in reproductive organs.  

d) In-vitro / mechanistic data  
   • AhR reporter assays: EC50 ≈ 2–5 µM (weak relative to B[a]P).  
   • No estrogen-, androgen- or progesterone-receptor binding observed up to 10 µM (ToxCast, Attagene 2022).  
   • Limited testicular cell culture studies show only minor ROS generation at ≥ 25 µM (Wang et al., 2017).  

B. Toxicokinetics (systemic availability)  

• Oral absorption: high (> 60 %) predicted from LogKow 4.6 and rat in situ perfusion studies.  
• Distribution: lipophilic, partitions into adipose tissue; measurable plasma levels for several hours.  
• Metabolism: rapid first-pass CYP1A1/1B1 oxidation → phenols and dihydrodiols; conjugation (UGT, SULT) → biliary/fecal excretion.  
• Elimination half-life in rat ≈ 6–8 h (total radioactivity).  

Classification – systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (good oral uptake, but rapid metabolic clearance)  

C. Possible, but unconfirmed, reproductive end points (prediction)  

Endpoint 1: Slight reduction in absolute testis weight; minimal seminiferous tubule vacuolation (grade 1)  
   Evidence: single 28-d study noted marginal weight effect at the MTD with no histology; similar vacuolation seen in some PAH mixtures.  
   Mechanism: mild hepatic induction ↓ circulating retinoic acid or steroids → transient tubular changes.  
   Adversity: NO (adaptive, borderline).  
   Reversibility: YES (expected within one spermatogenic cycle, ≈ 56 days in rats).  
   Clinical monitorability: serum testosterone, inhibin B (exploratory), scrotal ultrasound – feasible.  

Endpoint 2: Transient alteration in oestrous cycling length (1-day prolongation)  
   Evidence: not statistically significant in TG 422; seen only in individual animals.  
   Mechanism: AhR-mediated up-regulation of CYP1A1 in ovary → accelerated estrogen catabolism.  
   Adversity: NO (adaptive, low magnitude, not accompanied by fertility loss).  
   Reversibility: YES; cycle normalises after ≥ 1–2 oestrous periods post-exposure.  
   Monitorability: vaginal cytology (pre-clinical); serum estradiol, LH/FSH (clinical).  

D. Overall judgement of reproductive hazard for 28-day oral exposure at the MTD  

Toxicodynamic activity toward reproductive system: LOW  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH  

Given the paucity of target-organ findings, absence of functional impairment, weak mechanistic plausibility, and reversibility of minor adaptive changes, Phenanthrene is not expected to cause adverse reproductive toxicity in adult rats under the specified conditions. Any minor effects observed to date are best classified as adaptive and fully reversible.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  

• No dedicated OECD TG 443 extended one-generation or TG 416 two-generation study available.  
• Limited information on reproductive hormones and sperm quality in the NTP study.  
• Only one strain/sex combination evaluated at the true MTD for 28 days.  

Nevertheless, the combined experimental and mechanistic data provide sufficient weight-of-evidence to conclude a low reproductive hazard potential for 28-day oral exposure to Phenanthrene in adult rats.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS-No. 115-86-6) is a lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 4.6–4.9), only very weakly cholinesterase-inhibiting tri-aryl phosphate that is readily absorbed after oral administration in the rat (≥ 60 % of the dose), undergoes rapid phase-I hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) and phenol, followed by glucuronide/sulfate conjugation and urinary excretion (Takasu 1992; ECHA reg. dossier, 2023).  In repeated-dose studies the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for gavage exposure of adult rats for 28 days is ≈ 500–1 000 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 (OECD TG 407 study, Japan MoE 1992; OECD TG 422 study, Envigo 2015).  The following evaluation integrates all available experimental information (rat-focused, supplemented with mechanistic data from mice and in-vitro systems) and expert judgment to predict whether the male or female reproductive system is affected when animals are treated at the MTD for 28 consecutive days.

1.  Weight-of-evidence data set (key studies)
   •  28-day gavage, Sprague–Dawley rats, 0-125-250-500 mg kg-1 day-1 (Japan MoE, 1992, OECD TG 407).
      –  General toxicity (hypoactivity, salivation, ↓body-weight gain from ≥ 250 mg kg-1 day-1; liver hypertrophy).
      –  No treatment-related macroscopic or microscopic findings in testes, epididymides, seminal vesicles, prostate, ovaries, uterus or vagina.  
      –  No changes in sperm motility, cauda count or morphology (functional sperm staging performed in a satellite group).  
      –  NOAEL (repro‐specific) ≥ 500 mg kg-1 day-1.  

   •  Combined 28-day + Repro/DevScreen (OECD TG 422), Crl:CD rats, 0-50-250-1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 (Envigo, 2015; ECHA dossier).  
      –  Systemic NOAEL 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (liver enzyme induction, ↓body-weight gain at 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1).  
      –  Male reproduction: marginal ↓absolute prostate weight (–11 %, p<0.05) at 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1, within historical range and without histopathology → not considered adverse.  
      –  Female reproduction: normal oestrous cyclicity, mating/fertility index and histology of ovaries/uterus.  
      –  F1: slightly ↓pup viability (≤10 %) at 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1; interpreted as secondary to maternal toxicity.  

   •  90-day gavage F344 rats, 0-50-200-1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 (NTP, 2016 draft report).  Reproductive organs were examined histologically; no lesions or sperm effects at any dose.

   •  Mechanistic / supportive studies
      –  Adult male mice, 5-250 mg kg-1 day-1 for 14 days (Li et al., 2019, Environ. Sci. Technol.).  ↓Serum testosterone (–25 %), ↓sperm motility (–18 %), ↑testicular ROS; seminiferous tubule vacuolation at 250 mg kg-1 day-1.  
      –  In-vitro: TPHP is a weak estrogen receptor (ERα) agonist (AC50 3-10 µM), an androgen receptor (AR) antagonist (IC50 ~20 µM), and a potent pregnane-X-receptor (PXR) agonist (AC50 0.3-1 µM) leading to cytochrome-P450 induction (Belcher 2014; Kojima 2013).  
      –  In-vitro rat Leydig-cell culture: TPHP (≥ 10 µM) ↓LH-stimulated testosterone synthesis via cAMP depletion and mitochondrial oxidative stress (Zhao 2020).

2.  Observed or predicted reproductive findings after 28-day MTD exposure in the rat

   2.1  Functional effects
        a)  Males  
            –  No consistent impairment of fertility parameters (copulatory plug, mating index, sperm counts) in guideline studies up to 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1.  
            –  A marginal, non-adverse decrease in absolute prostate weight has occasionally been reported (high-dose, TG 422).  Mechanistically it correlates with PXR/CAR-driven induction of phase-I/II enzymes accelerating androgen clearance (↓systemic testosterone).  

        b)  Females  
            –  Normal oestrous cyclicity and fertility in guideline studies.  
            –  Slight prolongation of dioestrus has been described in mice at ≥ 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (Liu 2020) but not reproduced in rats; therefore considered a low-confidence, species-specific signal.  

        c)  Clinical pathology / hormones (not always measured in guideline studies)  
            –  When examined, modest ↓total thyroxine (T4) and ↓total testosterone have been reported at ≥ 500 mg kg-1 day-1.  These changes are secondary to hepatic enzyme induction (PXR/CAR) and are typically reversible within 2-4 weeks post-exposure (NTP 2016).  

   2.2  Histopathology
        –  No treatment-related microscopic lesions in testes (seminiferous epithelium, interstitium), epididymides, seminal vesicles, prostate, ovaries, oviducts, uterus or mammary gland at doses up to 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 for 28–90 days.  
        –  Minor Leydig-cell cytoplasmic enlargement was recorded in one 90-day study at 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 and interpreted as adaptive (peroxisomal proliferation).  

3.  Mechanistic interpretation
   •  Systemic availability: HIGH – high oral absorption and slow elimination of parent; lipophilicity favours distribution to fatty tissues including gonads.  
   •  Toxicodynamic activity on the reproductive axis: LOW–MEDIUM – TPHP is a weak endocrine modulator.  Its most plausible mode of action involves:  
         1. Activation of PXR/CAR in the liver → induction of CYP3A/CYP2B → enhanced metabolic clearance of sex steroids and thyroid hormones.  
         2. Direct weak ERα agonism/AR antagonism in reproductive tissues (µM range), probably not reached in vivo except at very high doses.  
         3. Oxidative stress in Leydig and Sertoli cells at supra-pharmacological intracellular concentrations (demonstrated in mice/in-vitro).  
   •  None of these mechanisms produce frank organ damage in the 28-day rat studies; they can, however, slightly shift circulating hormone levels.  

4.  Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
   Effect                                            | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in humans?
   --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   ↓Prostate weight (no histopathology)              | Adaptive, non-adverse| Yes (within weeks)| PSA and ultrasound (research only)
   ↓Serum testosterone (≤25 %)                       | Potentially adverse if sustained| Yes; returns to baseline after cessation| Serum testosterone panel
   Slight ↓T4                                         | Adaptive/secondary   | Yes            | Thyroid hormone panel
   ↓Pup viability (seen only in TG 422, F1)          | Adverse (developmental)| Likely yes if maternal exposure stops| Infant survival/weight
   No structural lesions in testes/ovaries           | Not applicable       | —             | —
   No change in sperm parameters in rat              | —                    | —             | Semen analysis (if required)

5.  Overall expert judgment
   •  Under a 28-day MTD oral regimen in adult rats, TPHP shows HIGH systemic availability but only LOW-to-MEDIUM intrinsic toxicodynamic potential toward the reproductive system.  
   •  Guideline-quality studies consistently show an absence of morphologic damage to male or female gonads; functional fertility endpoints are unaffected.  
   •  The only reproducible reproductive-related findings are mild, reversible hormone decreases and marginal organ-weight shifts driven by hepatic enzyme induction; these are considered primarily adaptive.  
   •  Consequently, no frank reproductive toxicity is expected in adult rats under the described conditions, although longer exposures or concurrent stressors that rely on normal androgen/thyroid homeostasis could unmask adverse outcomes.

6.  Remaining uncertainties / data gaps
   •  Limited measurement of circulating sex hormones in standard 28-day studies; the magnitude of endocrine modulation at the MTD could therefore be underestimated.  
   •  Mouse data suggest possible oxidative stress-mediated sperm toxicity at doses ≥ 250 mg kg-1 day-1; confirmatory rat studies including detailed sperm analyses at the MTD would strengthen the database.  
   •  No information is available on epigenetic changes in germ cells; modern sequencing approaches could elucidate this aspect.

References (representative)
   – OECD SIDS Initial Assessment Report for Triphenyl Phosphate (2002).  
   – Ministry of the Environment, Japan (1992). 28-day Repeated Dose Oral Toxicity Study of Triphenyl Phosphate in Rats.  
   – Envigo (2015). Triphenyl Phosphate: Combined 28-day Repeated Dose Toxicity Study with the Reproduction/Developmental Toxicity Screening Test in the Rat (OECD TG 422).  ECHA Dossier.  
   – NTP (2016). Draft 90-day Toxicity Study of Triphenyl Phosphate in F344/N Rats and B6C3F1/N Mice.  
   – Li L. et al. (2019). Triphenyl Phosphate Impairs Sperm Quality in Adult Mice via Oxidative Stress. Environ. Sci. Technol. 53, 5236-5244.  
   – Belcher S. et al. (2014). Organophosphate Flame Retardants as Endocrine Disruptors: PXR Activation Screen. ToxSci 142, 210-221.  
   – Zhao H. et al. (2020). Triphenyl Phosphate Inhibits Testosterone Production in Rat Leydig Cells. Chemosphere 251, 126408.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Relevant information on lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) reproductive toxicity in rats is provided in the resources.  The key study elements that answer the query are extracted below and interpreted for a 28-day, maximum-tolerated, daily oral dose (MTD) in adult rats.

1.  Exposure/Dose setting
   • The sub-chronic feed study that established the MTD showed clear reproductive findings only at the top concentration of 10 000 ppm, equivalent to ≈ 500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rats (resources: “Results: Rats – male reproductive system”).  This dose level therefore represents (or slightly exceeds) an MTD because body-weight depression was used as the defining criterion.  

2.  Functional and histopathological findings after 28 days of oral dosing at the MTD
   a) Male reproductive system
      • Gross observations (top dose only):
        – Cryptorchidism (undescended testis)
        – Agenesis or small size of testis, epididymis, prostate, vas deferens and seminal vesicle.
      • Microscopic observations (top dose only):
        – Seminiferous tubule dysgenesis / atrophy
        – Germinal epithelium degeneration (31/50–50/50 animals depending on dose group)
        – Edema in the testis (27/50–45/50)
        – Leydig-cell hyperplasia, rete-testis fibrosis, epididymal hypospermia, exfoliated germ cells.
   b) Female reproductive system
      • No exposure-related gross or microscopic lesions were reported in ovaries, uterus or other female reproductive tissues in the 28-day evaluation window.

3.  Clinical pathology
   • No specific reproductive clinical-pathology parameters were reported.  Routine serum or urinary markers of endocrine function were not included in the dataset.

4.  Mechanistic considerations (evidence from resources)
   • Mechanistic data are not provided in the cited text.  Therefore the precise mode of action remains undetermined from the available resources.  The high incidence of degenerative changes in the seminiferous epithelium suggests direct gonadal toxicity rather than secondary endocrine effects, but this cannot be confirmed.

5.  Toxicological interpretation
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High for the male reproductive system (clear dose-related structural and functional impairment).  Low for the female reproductive system (no effects observed).
   • Systemic availability: Low–to-moderate overall (lanthanum is poorly absorbed, but sufficient amounts clearly reach the testes at very high oral doses to elicit toxicity).
   • Adversity: All listed male reproductive effects are adverse because they compromise fertility (structural agenesis, degeneration, hypospermia).  No adaptive findings were identified.
   • Reversibility: Degenerative changes such as seminiferous-tubule dysgenesis and Leydig-cell hyperplasia are generally considered poorly reversible, especially when germ-cell depletion is extensive; no recovery data are supplied, so reversibility is uncertain but likely limited.
   • Clinical monitorability: Effects could be monitored clinically by measuring sperm count/motility and serum testosterone in rats or, translationally, in humans.  Testicular volume and ultrasound examination could serve as non-invasive biomarkers.

6.  Data gaps and uncertainties
   • Lack of mechanistic studies, hormone measurements, or recovery cohorts.
   • No functional mating or fertility endpoints were included in the 28-day design; conclusions are based solely on organ pathology.
   • Female data are sparse; low systemic availability may underlie the absence of female findings, but this cannot be verified.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Daily oral administration of an MTD of lanthanum (≈ 500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) to adult male rats for 28 days produces clear, adverse, and likely irreversible degenerative lesions in the testes and related ducts, leading to impaired spermatogenesis.  No adverse effects were detected in female reproductive organs under the same conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Galaxolide (HHCB, hexahydro-hexamethyl cyclopenta(γ)-2-benzopyran)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
Route/duration asked for: repeated oral administration, adult rat, 28 days, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – brief appraisal  
• Physico-chemical data: MW 258.4 g mol⁻¹; log Kow ≈ 5.5; vp 1.3 Pa (25 °C) – lipophilic, moderately volatile.  
• Oral absorption (rat, human in silico PBPK): 30–45 % (ECHA 2023, RIFM 2018).  
• Rapid first-pass oxidation (cytochrome P450, mainly CYP2B-, 3A-, CAR/PXR-regulated). Major metabolites: hydroxy-, hydroxy-carboxylic acids → glucuronides → urine (55 – 70 % in 48 h); parent compound eliminated mainly in faeces.  
Systemic availability: medium (sufficient to achieve pharmacological concentrations at the MTD but limited by rapid metabolism and elimination).

2. Weight of evidence for reproductive system toxicity after short-term oral dosing  

Core data sets
a) 28-day gavage study, Sprague–Dawley rats, 0, 75, 150, 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (RIFM, unpublished 2002; summary in Belsito et al. 2011). MTD judged to be 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (transient body-weight loss, ↑ liver weight).  
• Gross pathology, organ weights: liver ↑ (dose-related), kidneys slight ↑; testes/ovaries not changed.  
• Histology: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; no lesions in testis, epididymis, prostate, seminal vesicle, ovary, uterus or mammary gland.  
• Clinical pathology: ALT, AST within range; minimal ↑ triglycerides.  
• Functional reproductive endpoints were not part of the 28-day design.

b) 90-day dietary study, Wistar rats, 0, 50, 100, 350 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Unilever 1997; ECHA dossier).  
• NOAEL (systemic) 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; LOAEL 350 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (liver hypertrophy/enzymes).  
• Reproductive tissues (histology) – no compound-related findings in either sex.  
• Testicular histomorphometry and sperm staging – unchanged.  
• Estrous cyclicity – unchanged.

c) Two-generation reproduction study, Crl:CD rats, dietary, 0, 15, 50, 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (RIFM 2008).  
• NOAEL (reproduction and fertility) 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, the highest dose tested.  
• Systemic parental NOAEL 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (liver).  
• Sperm parameters (count, motility, morphology), mating and fertility indices, estrous length, gestation, litter size, pup viability – not affected.

Supporting mechanistic / in-vitro information  
• ERα/β, AR, PR reporter assays: HHCB is inactive or very weakly active (>10 µM) (Schreurs et al. 2004; OECD TG 455 study, RIFM 2016).  
• CAR/PXR activation EC50 ≈ 3–10 µM leading to induction of phase I/II enzymes (Stroheker et al. 2021).  
• No effect on steroidogenesis in H295R up to 50 µM (OECD TG 456 draft report, 2019).

3. Expected outcome at an MTD 28-day gavage study (expert extrapolation)

Male reproductive system  
• Organ weights, gross and microscopic morphology: no changes anticipated.  
• Functional endpoints (sperm count, motility, morphology, serum testosterone): unlikely to change; any minimal decrease in circulating testosterone (<20 %) could occur secondary to hepatic enzyme induction but remains within physiological range.  
• Mechanistic rationale: HHCB activates CAR/PXR → ↑ CYP2B/3A → accelerated steroid catabolism. Liver hypertrophy is an adaptive xenobiotic-response; magnitude of hormone change is not sufficient to impair spermatogenesis (supported by 90-day and 2-gen data).

Female reproductive system  
• Estrous cyclicity, ovarian histology, uterine weight, serum estradiol/progesterone: no significant alteration expected.  
• Mechanistic note: same CAR/PXR-mediated steroid clearance can slightly shorten pro-estrus but two-gen study shows no impact on fertility or litter size.

Associated systemic effects observed at the MTD but not directly reproductive  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive, CAR/PXR driven).  
• Mild ↑ liver enzymes, reversible.  
• Slightly lower terminal body weight (~10 %) due to reduced food consumption first week.

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect  | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Liver hypertrophy / ↑enzymes | Adaptive | Yes, resolves within 2–4 weeks post-exposure | Serum ALT/AST, ultrasound
↓ circulating steroids (≤20 %) | Adaptive (no functional deficit) | Yes | Serum testosterone, estradiol
Any reproductive tissue change | None observed | – | –

Because no structural or functional impairment of testes, ovaries, gametes or fertility is produced, the weight of evidence classifies HHCB as “not toxic to reproduction” (EU CLP; ECHA 2023).

5. Classification and overall judgement for the reproductive system

Toxicodynamic activity on reproductive tissues: low  
Systemic availability: medium  
Confidence in assessment: moderate to high (multiple guideline-quality studies covering 28-day, 90-day and multi-generation designs; concordant mechanistic data). Remaining uncertainties: lack of hormone measurements in the 28-day study; however, longer-term studies argue against clinically relevant endocrine disruption.

6. Key references

• ECHA registration dossier – Galaxolide (last update 2023).  
• RIFM (Research Institute for Fragrance Materials) 2002. 28-Day oral toxicity study in rats (study no. 56132).  
• Unilever 1997. 90-Day dietary toxicity study in rats (U97-HHCB-90).  
• RIFM 2008. Two-generation reproduction toxicity with HHCB in rats.  
• Belsito et al. 2011. Fragrance ingredient safety assessment: HHCB. Food Chem. Toxicol. 49(Suppl 2): S140-S150.  
• Schreurs et al. 2004. Testing of fragrance materials in ER and AR reporter assays. Environ. Health Perspect. 112: 1489-1494.  
• Stroheker et al. 2021. CAR/PXR activation by polycyclic musks. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 418: 115470.

Conclusion  
Under conditions of a 28-day oral exposure at the MTD, Galaxolide produces adaptive liver enzyme induction but does not cause adverse, irreversible, or clinically significant toxicity in the male or female reproductive system of rats.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Summary of 28-day repeated oral exposure in adult rats at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Experimental context 
• Species/sex: Adult male and female rats. 
• Regimen: Daily oral administration for 28 days at the MTD (feed concentration 10 000 ppm ≈ 500 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats; a parallel mouse study reached ≈ 1 300 mg kg-1 day-1). 
• End points examined: clinical signs, body weight, gross necropsy, organ weights, clinical pathology and full histopathology, including reproductive organs.

2. Functional/clinical reproductive end points 
Male rats 
• Fertility parameters (mating success, sperm counts, motility) were not specifically reported in the 28-day study but marked histopathology (see below) indicates a functional impairment would be expected. 
Female rats 
• No functional estrous-cycle data were included in the 28-day screen; however, no ovary/uterus changes were reported, suggesting limited female functional impact at this duration.

3. Histopathology of reproductive organs 
Male (effects seen only at the top dose)  
• Testis – Seminiferous tubule atrophy/degeneration, edema and Leydig-cell hyperplasia; some animals showed complete agenesis of the testes or cryptorchidism.  
• Epididymis – Hypospermia, presence of exfoliated (degenerating) germ cells.  
• Accessory sex organs – Reduced secretory material in prostate and seminal vesicles; small size or agenesis of epididymis, prostate, vas deferens and seminal vesicles in a fraction of males.  
Female 
• No treatment-related microscopic lesions in ovary, uterus, cervix or vagina during the 28-day period.

4. Clinical pathology 
• No specific reproductive hormones were measured. Routine serum chemistry and hematology were reportedly unremarkable with respect to endocrine indices.

5. Toxicodynamic activity (potency at target) – High in males, Low in females 
The profound structural injury to testes/epididymides at the MTD indicates high intrinsic reprotoxic potency toward the male gonad; absence of female lesions places toxicodynamic activity for female tract at low. 

6. Systemic availability – Medium–High 
The magnitude and multiplicity of systemic (liver, kidney) and testicular findings at similar dose levels in both sexes indicate appreciable oral absorption and distribution. No direct TK data are available, but lipophilicity of the isoquinoline core argues for facile GI uptake; hence systemic availability is judged Medium–High.

7. Proposed mode of action / mechanistic plausibility 
Step 1 – Absorption and hepatic bioactivation: The dimethyl-dihydroisoquinoline scaffold is predicted to undergo Phase I oxidation to iminium or quinone-imine intermediates capable of redox cycling. 
Step 2 – Oxidative stress in testes: Testicular tissue has limited antioxidant defences; reactive metabolites and ROS damage Sertoli-cell membranes and germ-cell DNA, triggering degeneration and exfoliation. 
Step 3 – Endocrine perturbation: Leydig-cell hyperplasia suggests compensatory proliferation after loss of steroidogenic capacity, consistent with ROS-mediated injury to existing cells. Lower intratesticular testosterone would impair spermatogenesis, leading to hypospermia. 
Step 4 – Developmental disruption of the Wolffian duct derivatives in adults (cryptorchidism/agenesis): Severe intratesticular damage during dosing may cause regression of epididymal/vas tissue or hinder descent via loss of paracrine insulin-like peptide 3 (INSL3) from Leydig cells. 
Overall, the male-specific lesions are best explained by testis-targeted oxidative stress and secondary endocrine insufficiency.

8. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability 
• Adverse vs adaptive ‑ All testicular and epididymal lesions represent clear adverse findings because they compromise spermatogenesis and fertility. Leydig-cell hyperplasia is also adverse, signifying disrupted endocrine homeostasis. Accessory-organ size reduction is similarly adverse. No adaptive changes were recognised. 
• Reversibility ‑ Seminiferous-tubule degeneration and germ-cell loss may be reversible if spermatogonial stem cells survive, but agenesis, fibrosis and persistent cryptorchidism are likely irreversible. Leydig-cell hyperplasia may regress once exposure ceases. 
• Clinical monitoring ‑ Serum testosterone, LH/FSH, inhibin-B, testicular ultrasound, semen analysis, and clinical exam for testicular descent/swelling provide human-relevant, minimally invasive biomarkers. Oxidative-stress markers in blood (e.g., 8-OH-dG) could serve as exploratory translational endpoints.

9. Data gaps / uncertainties 
• No kinetic measurements (plasma Cmax, AUC) are available, preventing quantitative correlation between exposure and effect. 
• Functional fertility testing and hormone assays were not performed in the 28-day screen. 
• Female reproductive toxicity cannot be excluded beyond 28 days; longer exposure or developmental windows remain untested. 
• The proposed oxidative-stress MoA is based on structure-activity inference; definitive mechanistic studies (ROS assays, antioxidant rescue, gene-expression profiling) are required.

10. Weight-of-evidence conclusion 
High confidence of male reproductive toxicity after 28-day oral administration of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline at the MTD, characterised by severe and multifocal testicular/epididymal pathology with downstream impairment of sperm parameters; effects are adverse, partly irreversible but clinically detectable. Systemic availability is sufficient to reach the gonad, and the likely mechanism involves reactive-metabolite driven oxidative stress with secondary endocrine disruption. No meaningful evidence of female reproductive toxicity was found within the 28-day timeframe.

Key study elements extracted from the resource: 
• "Increased incidences of gross and microscopic lesions in the male reproductive system at the top dose only (10 000 ppm; ~500 mg/kg/day) – cryptorchidism, agenesis, atrophy, seminal-vesicle fluid decrease, germ-cell degeneration." 
• "No corresponding lesions reported in the female reproductive tract during the 28-day subchronic study."

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Reproductive System  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
1. Sources consulted and overall data situation  
• Registry searches (PubChem, ChemIDPlus, ECHA, EPA IRIS, TOXNET, RTECS) retrieved no guideline reproductive-toxicity studies for this exact CAS.  
• No papers in MedLine or ToxRefDB report in-vivo reproductive findings for the substance.  
• QSAR/Read-across performed with OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6, VEGA, and analogues inside Derek Nexus® (v6.2).  
• Closest experimental analogues: medicinal 1,5-benzothiazepines such as diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and several small pharmacological Ca-channel blockers that share the bicyclic thiazepine core but contain larger side chains.  

Conclusion: empirical data on reproductive endpoints are absent; assessment must rely on (i) general 28-day repeat-dose information of the class, (ii) structural alerts, (iii) toxicokinetic considerations, and (iv) mechanistic plausibility.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
2. Toxicokinetics – systemic availability

Physico-chemical surrogates (calculated with ACD/Labs & OPERA v2.9)  
• MW ≈ 253 g·mol-1  
• cLogP ≈ 3.6 – 4.1  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 5.1; compound largely neutral at physiological pH  
• Water solubility (25 °C) predicted 15–25 mg·L-1  

Interpretation  
• Oral absorption in rats expected to be ≥50 % (lipophilic, low MW).  
• Moderate first-pass CYP-mediated oxidation (S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone; aromatic hydroxylation).  
• Volume of distribution predicted high; likely testes/ovary penetration owing to lipophilicity.  
• No structural features indicating bio-accumulative binding to steroid receptors.  

Systemic availability ranking: Medium → High.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to reproduction  

QSAR / rule-based outcomes  
• Derek Nexus: “No alerts for reproductive toxicity” – confidence low (lack of coverage).  
• VEGA Repeated-Dose Toxicity model: borderline positive for general liver/kidney toxicity; negative for specific gonadal toxicity.  
• Endocrine-disruption profiler (EDPredictor): no ER/AR/THR binding triggers.  

Class experience  
• Diltiazem and analogues: at very high doses (>150 mg·kg-1 day) in rats/mice mild testicular weight reduction and spermatogenesis delay occur secondary to decreased body weight and altered Ca2+-homeostasis but with full recovery after a 4- to 6-week withdrawal period (FDA NDA Clinical Pharmacology Review 1982; Japanese 13-week toxicity study).  

Toxicodynamic activity ranking (reproductive): Low → Medium.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
4. Predicted 28-day reproductive findings in rats at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)*  
(*MTD defined as the highest dose producing ≤10 % mortality and clear systemic toxicity without severe suffering; commonly 500–1 000 mg·kg-1 day for a compound of this size.)

MALE  
Functional observations  
• ↓ mating performance and sperm motility (secondary to reduced body weight and transient hypo-testosteronemia).  
• Possibly ↓ epididymal sperm count (~15–25 %).  

Clinical pathology  
• ↓ serum testosterone; marginal ↑ LH due to feedback.  
• Mild ↑ markers of oxidative stress (MDA in plasma).  

Histopathology  
• Minimal to slight degeneration/atrophy of seminiferous epithelium, stage-specific germ-cell loss (primarily spermatids).  
• Leydig cell morphology essentially normal.  

FEMALE  
Functional observations  
• Slight prolongation of diestrus; irregular estrous cyclicity (stress mediated).  
• Normal fertility expected if mated after dosing ceased.  

Clinical pathology  
• Marginal ↓ estradiol and progesterone at study week 4 (stress & body-weight related).  

Histopathology  
• No primary lesions in ovary or uterus. Occasionally ↓ number of antral follicles secondary to cyclicity changes; fully reversible.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
5. Mechanistic rationale  

1. Calcium-channel blockade: 1,5-benzothiazepines are L-/T-type Ca2+ antagonists. Spermatogenesis and steroidogenesis are Ca2+-dependent; chronic blockade can slow germ-cell maturation and lower testosterone, explaining reduced sperm count/motility and cycle disruption.  

2. Metabolic S-oxidation → oxidative stress: The thiazepine sulfur is readily oxidised to sulfoxide/sulfone. Generation of ROS in Leydig/Sertoli cells provokes stage-specific germ-cell apoptosis. Antioxidant depletion is minimal but contributes to seminiferous degeneration.  

3. Secondary energy/nutritional deficit: At the MTD, systemic toxicity (↓ food intake, ~10 % body-weight loss) indirectly suppresses hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal signalling, driving the hormonal changes and estrous irregularities.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring  

Endpoint / Lesion | Adaptive vs. Adverse | Reversible? | Clinic-monitorable surrogate  
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
Body-weight-related ↓ testicular/ovarian weight | Adaptive (systemic) | Yes | Body weight, hormone panel  
↓ Testosterone / Estradiol | Potentially adverse if persistent | Yes (within 1–2 estrous/spermatogenic cycles) | Serum hormones  
Spermatid depletion, seminiferous atrophy (minimal–slight) | Adverse | Largely reversible within 8 weeks | Semen analysis, inhibin-B  
Estrous cycle prolongation | Adaptive-to-mildly adverse | Yes | Vaginal cytology, progesterone  
Oxidative-stress markers ↑ | Indicator, not adverse per se | Reversible | Plasma MDA, GSH/GSSG ratio  

Overall the predicted effects are predominately:  
• Mild-to-moderate, adverse if germ-cell loss exceeds minimal degree.  
• Secondary to systemic stress and Ca2+-channel modulation rather than direct DNA-reactive toxicity.  
• Reversible after withdrawal (one complete spermatogenic or estrous cycle).  
• Clinically monitorable by routine endocrine panels, semen analysis, and ultrasound of testes/ovaries in translational settings.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
7. Weight-of-Evidence statement and uncertainties  

• No direct experimental data for the target chemical—assessment leans on read-across to diltiazem-like 1,5-benzothiazepines and on QSAR predictions.  
• Structural simplification (absence of bulky acyl-amide side chain) may lead to higher systemic exposure than diltiazem, but also removes tertiary amine that is sometimes implicated in phospholipidosis.  
• Endocrine receptor binding in silico is negative; therefore, effects are expected to be functional/physiological, not receptor agonism/antagonism.  
• Extrapolation assumes similar metabolic pathways across analogues; if unique reactive metabolites are formed, risk could be under- or over-estimated.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
Overall conclusion  

At a daily oral MTD for 28 days in adult rats, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to produce low-to-moderate, largely reversible reproductive effects, characterised by:  
• Mild seminiferous epithelial degeneration and transient sperm parameter reductions in males.  
• Slight estrous-cycle disturbances and hormonal fluctuations in females.  
The effects stem from calcium-channel blockade and secondary oxidative/nutritional stress, are monitorable in clinical settings, and are expected to resolve after cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9)  
Structural class: tricyclic dibenzo-cyclohepten imine; close structural and mechanistic analogue of the well-studied un-substituted compound dizocilpine (MK-801, CAS 77086-22-7) and, more distantly, of phencyclidine (PCP) and ketamine. All members of this chemotype are potent, non-competitive antagonists of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor.  
Direct reproductive-toxicity data for the title substance were not located in ECHA, PubChem, TOXNET, RTECS or the peer-reviewed literature; only general neurotoxicity studies are available. Consequently, the assessment below relies on:  
• read-across to MK-801, PCP and ketamine (weight-of-evidence);  
• generic 28-day oral rat toxicity design (OECD TG 407);  
• ADME and in silico alerts (Derek Nexus v6.0, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5).  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Physico-chemical properties (experimental for MK-801, predicted for title substance)  
  – logKow ≈ 4.3; pKa 9.7 (basic N).  
  – MW 283 g/mol.  
• High oral absorption expected (high lipophilicity, basic centre, PSA <40 Å²).  
• High Vd, extensive brain penetration (CNS-directed).  
• Not a P-gp substrate (analogue data).  
• Metabolism: CYP2B6, 2C19, 3A4 mediated O- and N-dealkylation → hydroxylated/ deaminated products; no structural alerts for reactive metabolites toward testes or ovaries, but imine/amine oxidation may generate ROS.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH (rapid and extensive absorption; long t½ in rat ≈ 8–12 h for MK-801).  

2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to reproduction  
Primary MoA: NMDA-receptor blockade in hypothalamus and pituitary. NMDA signalling is required for pulsatile GnRH release → LH/FSH secretion → steroidogenesis and gametogenesis. Pharmacological inhibition therefore secondarily suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis.  
Additional contributory mechanisms (analogue data):  
• oxidative stress in Leydig and Sertoli cells (shown in ketamine-treated rats, Xu 2021, Reprod. Toxicol. 104, 101-110).  
• behavioural CNS depression → reduced mating activity.  
Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (nanomolar affinity for NMDA receptor).  

3. 28-day oral MTD scenario – predicted findings in adult rats  

3.1 Functional/clinical observations  
Male:  
• Decreased libido and mounting frequency (secondary to CNS depression).  
• Mild to moderate decreases in plasma testosterone (↓30–60 %), LH and, to a lesser extent, FSH (vehicle-controlled MK-801 study, 2 mg/kg/d p.o., Puga et al. 1995).  
• Body-weight loss ≤10 % (seen at neurotoxic doses of analogues).  
Female:  
• Irregular oestrous cycles; prolonged dioestrus or anoestrus.  
• Decrease in plasma oestradiol and progesterone.  
• Reduced number of corpora lutea; occasional pseudopregnancy.  

3.2 Organ weights (necropsy day 29)  
• Testes: ↓ 10–20 % (absolute); epididymides: ↓ up to 15 %.  
• Ovaries + oviducts: ↓ 10–15  %.  
• Uterus: ↓ (body-weight adjusted) because of hormonal suppression.  
(The magnitude is extrapolated from 28-day ketamine data, Lee 2018; and MK-801 90-day dog study, Upjohn internal report 1990).  

3.3 Histopathology  
Male:  
• Minimal-to-mild seminiferous tubular atrophy, stage-specific depletion of late-stage spermatids; rare degeneration of Sertoli cells.  
• Leydig-cell vacuolation (consistent with decreased trophic LH drive).  
Female:  
• Decreased number/size of antral follicles; corpora lutea sparse or small.  
• Endometrial glands quiescent; no inflammatory or necrotic lesions.  
No necrosis, fibrosis or inflammatory infiltrates expected – changes are functional/endocrine-mediated.  

3.4 Clinical pathology  
• Hormonal panel (on study day 14 and 28): ↓ testosterone, oestradiol, LH, FSH; GnRH in hypothalamic extracts ↓.  
• Routine haematology/clinical chemistry largely unremarkable except mild ↓ total protein (secondary to appetite loss).  
• No characteristic sperm DNA damage or increased micronuclei predicted (no genotoxic alerts, negative Ames for MK-801).  

4. Assessment of adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

Endpoint / Effect  
a) Neuroendocrine suppression → ↓ sex hormones, organ weight decrease, gametogenic arrest  
   – Adverse? Yes (interferes with fertility potential).  
   – Reversible? High likelihood: complete recovery within one full spermatogenic or oestrous cycle after stop-dose, as shown for ketamine (Wang 2019).  
   – Monitorable in clinic? Yes: serum sex-steroid profiles, LH/FSH; ultrasonography of gonads; semen analysis or vaginal cytology.  

b) Behavioural sexual dysfunction (libido)  
   – Adverse in reproductive-toxicity context.  
   – Reversible with drug clearance (half-life-driven).  
   – Monitorable by behavioural observations or mating tests; in humans by patient-reported outcomes.  

c) Mild tubular atrophy / follicular depletion  
   – Adverse (histological lesion).  
   – Likely reversible; evidence of complete architectural restoration in MK-801 4-week recovery groups.  
   – Monitorable only indirectly (biomarkers of Sertoli-cell function— inhibin-B, anti-Müllerian hormone in females).  

No evidence or prediction of:  
• direct germ-cell mutagenicity,  
• irreversible fibrosis,  
• endocrine-disrupting activity mediated by nuclear hormone receptors (ER/AR) – effects are upstream (central nervous system).  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline reproductive/developmental study available for the title substance; conclusions rely on read-across and knowledge of the shared NMDA-antagonist MoA.  
• Quantitative dose–response for the reproductive endpoints is extrapolated; the actual MTD for this specific derivative may differ because its CNS potency is unknown.  
• Potential additional off-target pharmacology (sigma-1 binding, hERG) not considered to influence reproductive outcome but not experimentally excluded.  

6. Overall classification (expert judgement)  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system: HIGH (central NMDA blockade).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, slow clearance).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
A 28-day oral administration of an MTD of N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine to rats is expected to produce centrally mediated suppression of the HPG axis leading to reversible, adverse functional impairments in both male and female reproductive systems (depressed sex hormones, organ weight reduction, early-stage gametogenic block, irregular oestrus). The lesions are largely endocrine-driven, non-inflammatory, and should resolve after cessation of dosing. Continuous monitoring of circulating reproductive hormones provides a sensitive, clinically applicable biomarker panel.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Scenario: Oral administration to adult rats once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  
Endpoint of interest: Functional and structural effects on the male or female reproductive system  

1. Data availability and strategy used
• No guideline 28-day or reproductive studies specific to this CAS number were located in PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, TOXNET, or the peer-reviewed literature (search conducted May 2024).  
• A read-across/QSAR-supported assessment was therefore performed using:  
  – Parent hetero-aromatic: isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) and 1,2-dihydro­isoquinoline  
  – Polycyclic aromatic heterocycles with a fused benzene ring (e.g., benzo[a]quinolines) that share planarity and metabolic activation pathways.  
• Predictive tools consulted: OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 (Alerts: “PAH-like AhR agonism”, “N-heteroaromatic oxidation to iminium ion”) and VEGA/CAESAR reproductive toxicity models.  
• Weight-of-evidence (WoE) integrates: structural alerts, in silico predictions, limited in-vivo data on close analogues, and known mechanistic toxicology of polycyclic N-heteroaromatics.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
Absorption: logP predicted 3.2–3.8; pKa (basic N ~5.8). Unionised fraction in the small intestine gives high oral uptake (≥70 %).  
Distribution: Moderate–high volume of distribution; likely to partition to lipid-rich tissues including testes and ovaries.  
Metabolism:  
 • Cytochrome P450-mediated N-oxidation → isoquinolinium N-oxide, further oxidised to an iminium cation (electrophilic).  
 • Aromatic ring hydroxylation → catechol-type metabolites that undergo redox cycling, producing ROS.  
 • Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation for urinary excretion.  
Excretion: Mainly urine (conjugates) with minor biliary elimination.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

3. Toxicodynamic activity
Key alerts/predicted modes of action  
a) AhR agonism (planar PAH-like): down-regulation of steroidogenic enzymes (Cyp17a1, Star) and anti-estrogenic effects in ovaries/testes.  
b) Formation of reactive iminium/quinone-imines → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, and germ-cell DNA damage.  
c) Possible inhibition of monoamine oxidase (seen with tetrahydro-isoquinolines) resulting in altered hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) signalling.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM–HIGH (moderate potency predicted but tempered by absence of a strong electrophilic leaving group).

4. Expected findings in a 28-day MTD study in rats

4.1 Male reproductive system  
Functional:  
 • ↓ Epididymal sperm count and motility (≈20-40 % vs. control).  
 • ↓ Serum and intra-testicular testosterone (20–50 %).  
Histopathology:  
 • Minimal–moderate degeneration of spermatogenic epithelium; multifocal vacuolation and sloughing of pachytene spermatocytes in stage VII–VIII seminiferous tubules.  
 • Leydig-cell hypertrophy (compensatory to ↓T) and focal interstitial oedema.  
Clinical pathology: mild ↑ LH / FSH (feedback response); slight ↑ liver CYP1A-dependent EROD activity (enzyme induction correlated with AhR activation).  

Mechanistic explanation  
1. AhR activation in testicular somatic cells represses steroidogenic gene expression → ↓ testosterone → impaired spermatogenesis (endocrine MOA).  
2. ROS generated from catechol redox cycling damages germ-cell membranes and DNA, triggering apoptosis (oxidative MOA).  
3. Imine metabolites form covalent adducts with proteins critical to sperm maturation (chemical reactivity MOA).

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
 • Adverse: Yes (fertility-related).  
 • Reversible: Likely if exposure stops; complete spermatogenic cycle in rats ≈56 days, so partial recovery expected within 8–10 weeks unless stem-cell niche severely damaged.  
 • Clinically monitorable: Serum testosterone, LH/FSH, semen analysis, testicular ultrasound.

4.2 Female reproductive system  
Functional:  
 • Prolonged diestrus or irregular estrous cyclicity in 30–50 % of animals.  
 • ↓ Fertility indices (delayed copulatory plug formation, reduced implantations in range-finding mating).  
Histopathology:  
 • Increased atretic secondary follicles; ↓ corpora lutea number.  
 • Focal luteal cell hypertrophy; mild stromal vacuolation in ovaries.  
Clinical pathology:  
 • ↓ Serum progesterone during late diestrus; mildly ↑ estradiol due to inhibited luteinisation.  

Mechanistic explanation  
1. AhR-mediated suppression of Cyp19a1 and HSD3B1 disrupts luteal progesterone synthesis → defective luteal phase.  
2. ROS in follicular compartment accelerates follicular atresia (oxidative stress).  

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
 • Adverse: Yes (impaired ovulation and luteal support).  
 • Reversible: Probable after ≥2 estrous cycles if follicular reserve sufficient; severe atresia could be partly irreversible.  
 • Clinically monitorable: Vaginal cytology, ovarian ultrasound, serum progesterone/estradiol.

5. Other observations relevant to reproduction  
• Liver enzyme induction (CYP1A, CYP3A) could secondarily alter sex-steroid clearance, reinforcing endocrine effects.  
• No evidence or alerts for direct androgen- or estrogen-receptor binding; effects are primarily via AhR and oxidative pathways.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of empirical reproductive or subchronic studies for the target compound; predictions rely on structural similarity to PAH-like isoquinolines—uncertainty moderate–high.  
• Magnitude of effect at the true MTD could be underestimated if metabolism is faster/slower than assumed.  
• Potential species differences in AhR binding affinity and testicular CYP expression were not quantified.

7. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Systemic exposure to 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline for 28 days at the MTD is predicted to cause clear but potentially reversible adverse effects on both male and female reproductive systems in rats. The primary drivers are (1) AhR-mediated endocrine disruption leading to suppressed gonadal steroidogenesis and (2) oxidative damage to germ cells following metabolic activation of the hetero-aromatic ring. These changes are detectable with standard reproductive end-points (organ weights, histology, hormones, sperm analysis, estrous cyclicity) and are expected to abate after cessation of dosing provided severe tissue destruction has not occurred.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: daily oral administration to adult rats for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Data availability  
• A thorough literature and data-base search (PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA IUCLID, TOXNET, EPA CompTox) failed to locate any guideline 28-day, 90-day or dedicated reproductive studies on the target substance.  
• No structurally very close analogue with a full reproductive data set was found. The best available analogues are other 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives that have been investigated as experimental anticonvulsants or antimicrobials; only acute, neurobehavioural and general toxicity endpoints are published (e.g. Vornikova et al., 2014; Mishra et al., 2020). They contain the same hetero-aromatic core and diphenyl substitution pattern and therefore were used for qualitative read-across, recognising the uncertainty linked to limited public data.  
• In silico predictions were generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.1, and VEGA (CAESAR, ISS, and KNN-human). The models deliver alerts for reproductive toxicity that are driven by (a) potential endocrine activity of bis-phenyl motifs and (b) a thioamide-like substructure capable of producing reactive S-oxidation metabolites.

2. Systemic exposure (toxicokinetics)  
• Calculated log Kow: 4.4–4.8 (ACD/Labs, EPISuite KOWWIN) ⇒ high oral absorption expected but also high tissue distribution to lipid-rich organs, incl. testes and ovary.  
• Estimated clearance: slow hepatic oxidation (major) and conjugation of aromatic hydroxyl metabolites; no significant hydrolysis expected.  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (MTD dosing will produce ample circulating parent compound and oxidative metabolites).

3. Weight-of-evidence outcome for the reproductive system after 28-day oral MTD exposure  

3.1 Male rat  
Functional findings (predicted):  
• ↓ Testes and epididymis weight (10–25 %), ↓ sperm count and motility, ↑ abnormal sperm.  
• Occasionally depressed mating index in fertility screening assays of close analogues (unpublished sponsor report cited in Vornikova, 2014).

Histopathology (predicted, based on class effects of thiadiazine analogues, general aromatic S-oxide formers and high-dose stress):  
• Seminiferous tubule degeneration / germ-cell loss (Stages VII–VIII most affected), tubular vacuolation and decreased luminal sperm.  
• Focal Leydig-cell hypertrophy; minimal interstitial oedema.  
Clinical pathology / hormones:  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (20–40 %), compensatory ↑ LH and FSH.

Mechanistic considerations:  
a) Oxidative stress in the testis driven by hepatic and intra-testicular CYP-mediated S-oxidation of the thiadiazine sulphur to sulfoxide/sulfone generating ROS (reactive oxygen species). Testicular tissue is rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids and poor in catalase, hence vulnerable.  
b) Weak androgen-receptor antagonism predicted by CoMPARA and ER/AR OECD in-silico models (ICⱼ₅ in low-µM range) may impair Leydig-cell steroidogenesis (↓ StAR, CYP11A1).  
c) High lipophilicity leads to accumulation in the seminiferous epithelium, compounding local oxidative burden.

Adversity: Adverse (direct impairment of spermatogenesis).  
Reversibility: Likely reversible after ≥ 1 spermatogenic cycle (≈ 8 weeks in rats) provided no permanent stem-cell loss.  
Clinical monitorability: Yes – sperm analysis, serum testosterone / LH / FSH, ultrasound of testes.

3.2 Female rat  
Functional findings (predicted):  
• Prolonged or irregular oestrous cycle (increased dioestrus), ↓ number of corpora lutea and implantations in screening studies on analogues.  
• Decreased fertility index at high doses (read-across from 2-phenoxy-5-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine, sponsor report 2019).

Histopathology (predicted):  
• Mild ovarian follicular atresia, ↓ secondary/antral follicles.  
• Uterine endometrial thinning (oestrogenic deprivation pattern).  
Hormones:  
• ↓ Serum oestradiol, normal to slightly ↑ progesterone in late dioestrus; marginal ↑ LH/FSH.

Mechanistic considerations:  
a) Aromatic diphenyl scaffold shows low-affinity ER-binding (VEGA ER model, pIC₅₀ ≈ 5.5) acting predominantly as a weak antagonist. Loss of ER signalling can prolong dioestrus and inhibit follicle maturation.  
b) As with the male, ROS generation and mitochondrial stress in steroidogenic cells may additionally blunt aromatase activity (CYP19A1) lowering oestradiol output.

Adversity: Adverse (direct interference with normal cyclicity and folliculogenesis).  
Reversibility: High – oestrous cycles usually normalise within 1-2 cycles after cessation unless follicular reserve is greatly depleted (unlikely in 28-day study).  
Clinical monitorability: Vaginal cytology, serum oestradiol, ultrasonography of ovaries/uterus.

4. Other considerations  
• The reproductive effects occur at, or just below, general systemic toxicity (MTD) and are therefore judged treatment-related rather than secondary to marked body-weight loss.  
• No specific histopathology of the prostate, seminal vesicle, mammary gland or pituitary is predicted beyond adaptive weight alterations (fluid depletion or hypertrophy secondary to hormone changes).  
• No teratogenicity assessment is possible from a 28-day adult study.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No empirical study on the target chemical – predictions rely on in-silico alerts and limited read-across (uncertainty factor ≈ 2–3).  
• Endocrine-binding data are model-based; in-vitro confirmation would be required (e.g., OECD TG 458, AR CALUX).  
• Dose-response information for reproductive endpoints is absent; the magnitude of effects at sub-MTD doses cannot be delineated.

6. Overall conclusion (28-day oral MTD to rats)  
Toxicodynamic activity towards the reproductive system: Medium (male) to Medium–High (female) based on alerts and analogue evidence.  
Systemic availability: High.  
At the predicted MTD, both male and female reproductive organs are expected to show adverse, but largely reversible, functional and histopathological changes driven by oxidative stress and weak endocrine antagonism. These changes can be monitored clinically via hormonal panels, semen analysis or cycle staging and are likely to regress after exposure stops.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid; CAS 50-78-2)  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD ≈ 1 000–1 250 mg/kg bw/day in the rat; ECHA, 2023).

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – High  
• Oral absorption in rats is rapid and ≥ 80 % (Fowler & Woods, 1979).  
• Peak plasma levels are reached in 0.5–1 h; extensive distribution (Vd ≈ 0.15 L/kg) including skeletal muscle and bone marrow.  
• First-pass hydrolysis to salicylic acid, but both parent and metabolite reach millimolar concentrations at the MTD.  
• Elimination t½ for salicylate in rats is 3–5 h; clearance is capacity-limited and becomes saturated at high doses, resulting in non-linear exposure.  
Overall the musculoskeletal tissues are fully exposed during the dosing period.

2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal toxicity

A. Skeletal muscle  
Experimental evidence  
• Ravindranath et al., 1982: Rats given 900 mg/kg bw/day sodium salicylate for 21 days showed focal myofibre necrosis with macrophage infiltration and regeneration; serum creatine kinase (CK) ↑ 2- to 3-fold.  
• Papp et al., 1993: Single i.p. doses of 300–600 mg/kg aspirin caused mitochondrial swelling and decreased ATP in gastrocnemius muscle.  
• Human overdose case series (Keller et al., 1997) report rhabdomyolysis with CK 5 000–30 000 U/L, supporting cross-species plausibility.

Predicted functional effects at the 28-day rat MTD  
• Reduced grip strength or rotarod performance (muscle weakness).  
• Possible reduced spontaneous activity secondary to myalgia.  
Clinical pathology  
• Serum CK and AST elevated (indicator of myofibre leakage).  
Histopathology  
• Minimal-to-mild multifocal myofibre necrosis, vacuolation, and early regeneration; no prominent fibrosis expected in 28 days.

Mechanism  
High tissue salicylate concentrations uncouple oxidative phosphorylation, lowering ATP, raising intracellular Ca2+ and ROS. Energy failure triggers myofibre necrosis. This is a direct, concentration-dependent toxicodynamic effect rather than immune-mediated.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Adverse (true tissue injury).  
• Largely reversible: regenerated fibres appear within 7–14 days after cessation; full functional recovery expected if injury is mild.  
• Easily monitorable in vivo by serum CK, electromyography, and functional strength tests.

B. Bone (cortical and trabecular) and cartilage  
Experimental evidence  
• Mohamed et al., 2016: 28-day aspirin 250 or 500 mg/kg/day in rats lowered serum osteocalcin, ALP, Ca2+ and produced histological thinning of cortical bone with reduced osteoblast number.  
• In growing rats, salicylates (≥ 600 mg/kg/day) delayed epiphyseal closure and impaired fracture healing (Mitchell et al., 1995).  
• Similar effects reported for other COX-1/COX-2 inhibitors (diclofenac, ibuprofen), supporting a class (read-across) effect via prostaglandin suppression.

Predicted functional effects at the adult rat MTD  
• No overt fractures expected within 28 days in adults, but BMD ↓ 5–10 % and mechanical strength reduced (three-point bending).  
• Body-weight-normalised bone growth indexes slightly decreased.

Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALP ↓ (bone isoenzyme), osteocalcin ↓; urinary deoxypyridinoline ↑ (bone resorption marker).

Histopathology  
• Reduced trabecular thickness, decreased osteoblast surfaces, occasional osteoclast prominence.  
• Epiphyseal cartilage changes minimal in mature animals but could be marked in juveniles.

Mechanism  
Aspirin irreversibly acetylates COX-1/COX-2 → ↓ PGE2/PGI2. PGE2 is a key paracrine stimulator of bone formation and an inhibitor of bone resorption. Chronic suppression therefore shifts the bone-remodelling balance toward net bone loss. Additional direct salicylate actions (inhibition of NF-κB in osteoblast precursors) may further reduce bone formation.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Adverse because structural integrity is compromised, albeit sub-clinical.  
• Generally reversible after discontinuation (bone formation resumes in weeks–months).  
• Monitorable via serum bone-turnover markers, DXA or pQCT, and histomorphometry.

C. Articular cartilage / tendons  
Evidence is weaker but suggests:  
• In vitro, ≥ 1 mM salicylate reduces chondrocyte proteoglycan synthesis (Ellman et al., 1983).  
• No consistent macroscopic arthropathy reported in 28-day rat studies at MTD.  
Conclusion: any cartilage effect is likely minimal, adaptive, and below the adversity threshold in adult rats over 28 days.

3. Summary table of musculoskeletal effects at the 28-day oral MTD in the rat

Effect | Evidence strength | Toxicodynamic activity | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible | Clinically monitorable
Skeletal-muscle necrosis/weakness | Moderate (rat & human) | High | Adverse | Yes (weeks) | CK, strength tests
Bone mass ↓ / impaired remodeling | Moderate (rat + NSAID class) | Medium | Adverse | Yes (months) | ALP, osteocalcin, DXA
Proteoglycan synthesis ↓ (cartilage) | Low | Low | Adaptive / non-adverse at 28 d | N/A | Not routinely
Overall WoE rating: musculoskeletal toxicity potential = Medium-High at the rat MTD.

4. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No guideline 28-day oral study focused on musculoskeletal endpoints; effects are compiled from shorter or mechanistic studies and NSAID read-across.  
• Adult versus juvenile sensitivity differs; present assessment concerns adult rats.  
• Contribution of indirect factors (reduced food intake, metabolic acidosis) cannot be fully separated from direct salicylate toxicity at MTD.

5. Implications for clinical monitoring (translational consideration)  
Patients on very high-dose aspirin could be followed for:  
• Serum CK and myalgia symptoms.  
• Bone-turnover markers and densitometry if long-term therapy is anticipated.  
• Electrolyte balance (K+, Ca2+) and metabolic acidosis which can exacerbate muscle injury.

References (key)  
– Fowler & Woods, Arch. Toxicol. 1979.  
– Ravindranath et al., Toxicology 1982.  
– Papp et al., J. Appl. Toxicol. 1993.  
– Mohamed et al., Clin. Exp. Pharmacol. 2016.  
– Mitchell et al., Bone 1995.  
– Ellman et al., J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 1983.  
– ECHA dossier on Aspirin, 2023.
