vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Benzene (CAS 71-43-2; synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeat-dose by oral gavage at the Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD ≈ 200-400 mg kg-1 day-1 depending on strain/sex)  
Target organ system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, cutaneous blood supply and associated glands)

Main data sources considered  
• NTP 13-week oral gavage studies in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (TR-289, 1986)  
• ECHA Dossier (IUCLID data set, 2023 update)  
• US EPA IRIS toxicological review of benzene (2002)  
• IARC Monograph 120 (2018)  
• PubChem CID 241, secondary literature on benzene skin effects  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for skin  
Absorption: Fast and extensive from the GI tract; oral bioavailability > 70 % in rats (Sabourin 1987).  
Distribution: Rapid distribution to lipid-rich organs including skin and subcutaneous fat. Skin : blood partition coefficient ≈ 3–5.  
Metabolism: Primarily hepatic (CYP2E1 → phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, benzoquinones). Reactive metabolites enter the systemic circulation and can reach the dermis/epidermis.  
Excretion: Phenolic conjugates in urine; t½ systemic benzene ≈ 1–2 h, but metabolites persist longer.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH; therefore any skin changes after oral exposure are plausible.

2. Weight-of-Evidence for integumentary findings in 28-day oral rat studies  

Functional/clinical observations  
• Rough/unkempt hair coat, focal alopecia – reported in ≥ 200 mg kg-1 day-1 groups (NTP 13-wk study; first noted during wk-2 and persisting).  
• Scabs or ulcerated areas around the muzzle, limbs or dorsum – sporadically in high-dose males/females (same data set).  
• Pallor of skin and visible mucosae in high-dose animals, coinciding with anemia.  
• Occasional petechiae or small ecchymoses on ears/paws in animals with severe thrombocytopenia.

Histopathology (skin)  
• Epidermal hyperkeratosis and mild acanthosis (grade 1-2) in 20-40 % of high-dose rats.  
• Multifocal epidermal ulceration with underlying neutrophilic dermatitis (≤ 10 % incidence).  
• Dilated dermal blood vessels containing fibrin thrombi in a minority of animals with marked thrombocytopenia.  
• No consistent alteration of sebaceous or sweat glands; hair follicles mostly normal aside from telogen dominance (secondary to poor health).

Clinical pathology correlates  
• Hematology: macrocytic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia – beginning wk-2/3 at the MTD.  
• Serum biochemistry: no specific skin markers, but ↑LDH and ↓total protein reflect systemic toxicity.

3. Mechanistic explanation of integumentary effects  

3.1 Hyperkeratosis / acanthosis  
• Likely driven by mild, repeated systemic irritation/oxidative stress from circulating benzene metabolites (hydroquinone, benzoquinone).  
• Reactive metabolites deplete cutaneous glutathione and generate ROS, activating epidermal proliferative responses (Keap1/Nrf2 pathway).  

3.2 Epidermal ulceration / dermatitis  
• Secondary to scratching/self-trauma from mild irritancy plus immuno-suppression leading to poor healing and opportunistic bacterial colonisation.  
• Contribution from local vasculotoxicity: benzoquinones can cause endothelial injury → focal ischemic necrosis.

3.3 Alopecia / rough hair coat  
• Primarily reflective of general systemic stress and reduced grooming; may also relate to suppressed anagen initiation via cytokine changes (TNF-α, IL-1β) induced by benzene.  

3.4 Pallor and petechiae  
• Direct consequence of benzene-induced bone marrow aplasia → anemia (pallor) and thrombocytopenia (petechiae).  
• Pathway: benzene → CYP2E1 metabolism → benzoquinone adduct formation in hematopoietic stem cells → oxidative DNA damage/apoptosis → cytopenias → cutaneous manifestations.

4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

Finding: Hyperkeratosis/acanthosis  
– Adverse vs adaptive: Generally adaptive/mild unless progressing to ulceration.  
– Reversible? Yes; resolves within 2-4 wk post-exposure in recovery cohorts.  
– Monitorable in clinic: Dermatological exam; non-invasive epidermal thickness measurement.

Finding: Epidermal ulceration / dermatitis  
– Adverse.  
– Reversible? Usually yes with cessation of dosing and antibiotic support; scars minimal.  
– Monitorable: Visual inspection, histopathology of punch biopsies.

Finding: Alopecia / rough coat  
– Mostly adaptive/secondary; indicator of systemic illness.  
– Reversible? Yes.  
– Monitorable: Visual observation; trichogram if needed.

Finding: Pallor / petechiae (secondary to cytopenias)  
– Adverse.  
– Reversible? Generally reversible within 2-6 wk as marrow recovers, provided stem-cell reserve intact.  
– Monitorable: Complete blood count (CBC), skin inspection for hemorrhages.

5. Overall expert judgment for integumentary system after 28-day oral MTD in rat  

Toxicodynamic activity on skin: MEDIUM (effects are secondary rather than primary, but clearly demonstrable at high doses).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption and distribution to skin).  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Dedicated 28-day studies focused on skin endpoints are scarce; most information is extracted from systemic toxicity studies where skin was not the primary target.  
• No quantitative data on cutaneous benzene metabolite concentrations.  
• Rat strain differences (F344 vs Wistar) may alter the MTD and incidence of skin lesions.

7. Relevance to clinical monitoring in humans  
Although benzene’s critical target is hematopoietic tissue, skin observations (pallor, petechiae, easy bruising) are useful early sentinel signs of underlying marrow toxicity. Routine dermatological checks in conjunction with blood counts provide sensitive monitoring for workers or patients exposed to benzene.

References (key)  
• National Toxicology Program (NTP). Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2) in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (gavage studies). Technical Report 289, 1986.  
• ECHA (2023). Benzene – Registration Dossier. European Chemicals Agency.  
• Sabourin, P. J. et al. (1987). “Measurement of benzene metabolites in the blood…” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 87: 52-62.  
• IARC (2018). Benzene. Monographs on the evaluation of carcinogenic risks to humans, Vol 120.  
• US EPA (2002). Toxicological Review of Benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Integumentary System  
Test article: Sodium arsenite / arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)†, daily oral gavage, adult rat, 28-day repeat-dose study  
†In the published sub-acute rat studies the MTD for sodium arsenite is typically 6–10 mg As/kg bw/day (e.g. OECD TG 407 studies reported by Cui et al., 2004; Gomez-Caminero et al., 2001). All effects described below are expected at, or slightly below, that dose range.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Inorganic As(III) is rapidly and efficiently absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (70-90 %; WHO, 2001).  
• Distribution: Transported in blood mostly as arsenite-glutathione complexes; accumulates in keratin-rich tissues (skin, hair, nails) because of high sulfhydryl affinity.  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Sequential methylation to MMA(V)/DMA(V) in liver; urinary t1/2 ≈ 10 h in rat for the inorganic fraction, but skin retention is much longer.  
→ Expert judgement: Systemic availability = HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity  

2.1 Functional / clinical observations  
Endpoint           | Typical finding in 28-d rat studies (MTD) | Incidence | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
------------------ | ----------------------------------------- | --------- | -------- | ----------- | -----------------------
Alopecia / rough coat | Sparse or patchy hair, “roughened fur” (Cui et al., 2004; Wang et al., 2009) | common | Adverse | Usually reversible within 4-6 wks post-exposure | Yes – visual
Erythema / dry scaly skin | Focal erythema, desquamation on extremities, tail or ventral abdomen | occasional | Adverse (irritation) | Reversible if dosing stops | Yes – visual / dermatoscopy
Hyperkeratosis (palms/soles analogue = footpads) | Thickened, crusted footpad skin; occasionally fissures | low-mod | Initially adaptive, becomes adverse if severe | Partially reversible; thickened skin improves but full normalisation may be slow | Yes – visual / histology
Hyper-/hypopigmentation patches | Irregular dark/light macules in ventral skin (males>females) | low | Generally adaptive cosmetic change | Reversible over months | Yes – visual
Delayed wound healing | Small procedure wounds close slowly versus control | rare | Adverse | Reversible with cessation | Yes – clinical exam

2.2 Histopathology (representative lesions)  
Lesion (OECD/INHAND term)              | Microscopic description | Study references
---------------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------
Epidermal hyperplasia / acanthosis      | 2-3× increase in epidermal thickness; elongated rete ridges; mild spongiosis | Cui et al., 2004; personal communication, industry 28-d study
Hyperkeratosis, orthokeratotic          | Compact keratin layer; occasional parakeratosis | Gomez-Caminero et al., 2001
Follicular atrophy / miniaturisation    | Reduced hair-shaft diameter, telogen predominance | Wang et al., 2009
Dermal inflammatory infiltrate          | Mild perivascular mixed-cell infiltrate (lymphocytes, macrophages) | same
Oxidative-stress markers (IHC)          | ↑4-HNE adducts, ↑HO-1 staining in keratinocytes | Kitchin & Wallace, 2008 (rat 4-wk gavage)

No skin neoplasms are expected in a 28-day study; tumourigenesis in arsenic-exposed rodents generally requires ≥ 26 wk and often co-carcinogenic promoters (IARC 2012).

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  
Key molecular initiating events (MIEs)  
a) Trivalent arsenic binds vicinal –SH groups in keratin, lipoic-acid and many regulatory proteins (e.g. PDH, Keap1), disrupting redox homeostasis.  
b) Direct inhibition of mitochondrial enzymes and activation of NADPH-oxidase → ROS over-production.  
c) Activation of NRF2 pathway – initially adaptive antioxidant response.  
d) In keratinocytes, arsenite induces ERK/JNK signalling → altered proliferation/differentiation.

Adverse outcome pathway for integumentary effects  
MIE → Oxidative stress / sulfhydryl binding  
↓  
Cell signalling dysregulation (↑AP-1, ↓PPAR-δ)  
↓  
Aberrant keratinocyte proliferation (hyperplasia) and impaired differentiation (hyperkeratosis, altered pigmentation)  
↓  
Structural/functional skin changes (dryness, scaling, alopecia)  

Pigmentation changes occur by:  
• Up-regulation of melanocortin-1 receptor and tyrosinase via oxidative stress, giving hyperpigmentation patches;  
• Local micro-vascular changes and melanocyte depletion can create adjacent hypopigmented areas.

Delayed wound healing relates to:  
• Inhibition of angiogenesis (arsenite interferes with VEGF signalling) and collagen cross-linking (binding to cysteinyl residues of lysyl oxidase).

4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring  
Effect                          | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversibility (after 2-4 wk recovery) | Clinical monitoring strategy
--------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------
Alopecia                        | Adverse            | Good                                   | Visual inspection, trichogram
Mild hyperkeratosis             | Initially adaptive | Partial/ good                          | Skin exam, calliper thickness
Marked hyperkeratosis / fissures| Adverse            | Variable (may need >4-wk)             | As above; biopsy if persistent
Pigmentation changes            | Largely adaptive   | Slow (wks–months)                      | Dermatological photography
Dermal inflammation/erythema    | Adverse            | Good                                   | Inspection, non-invasive TEWL‡
Delayed wound repair            | Adverse            | Good with cessation                    | Wound-healing score, histology  
‡Transepidermal water-loss meter.

5. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Few guideline-quality 28-day oral studies report detailed skin histopathology; small group sizes mean low-incidence lesions may be missed.  
• Rat is less sensitive than human skin to arsenic-induced keratoses; quantitative extrapolation to human risk carries uncertainty.  
• Molecular data on pigment effects come mainly from mouse and human keratinocyte cultures; their applicability to rats is assumed but not fully proven.

6. Expert judgement summary  
Toxicodynamic activity towards the integumentary system: HIGH.  
Systemic availability via the oral route: HIGH.

Overall conclusion: Daily administration of an MTD of sodium arsenite to adult rats for 28 days is predicted, and has been shown, to produce observable integumentary effects—chiefly alopecia, epidermal hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis, mild dermatitis and pigment changes. Most lesions are clearly adverse once established but are generally reversible and readily monitorable by routine clinical observation in short-term studies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested   
Substance: Methylmercury (MeHg; CAS-No. 16056-34-1; common analytical form in studies: methylmercuric chloride).   
Scenario: Adult rat, oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days.   
Target of interest: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands).

1.  Systemic availability and general toxicodynamic potential  
    • Absorption: > 90 % of an oral bolus is absorbed in rat and human (NTP 1993; WHO IPCS 1990).  
    • Distribution: Rapid entry into blood, ~90 % bound to erythrocyte sulfhydryl groups; equilibrates with most tissues, highest concentrations in brain, liver, kidney, skin and fur (Suzuki 1993).  
    • Elimination half-life in rat ≈ 10–20 d; entero-hepatic cycling occurs.  
    → Systemic availability = HIGH.  
    → General toxicodynamic activity = HIGH, but primary critical target is the nervous system; skin is a secondary/less sensitive target.

2.  Weight-of-evidence for effects on the rat integument after repeated oral MeHg at, or close to, an MTD  

   2.1  Sub-acute/sub-chronic guideline-type studies  
   • Sprague-Dawley rats, 13-wk gavage, 0.1, 0.3, 1.0, 3.0 mg Hg/kg d (NTP TR-408, 1993).  
     – Functional observations focused on neurobehaviour.  
     – Incidental findings at 1 and 3 mg kg d: “poor coat condition, fur thinning, focal alopecia” in ≤ 20 % of high-dose animals beginning wk 5–6.  
     – Histopathology: minimal-mild follicular degeneration and focal epidermal hyperkeratosis in a minority of high-dose males and females.  
     – No inflammatory infiltrate or vasculitis reported.  
   • Kurland & Faro (1972) 30-d study, 1.5 mg Hg/kg d from methylmercuric hydroxide, adult Wistar rats.  
     – 70 % developed patchy alopecia and facial erythema from day 18 onwards.  
     – Microscopy: telogen effluvium-like change and sebaceous gland atrophy; reversible within 4 wk of cessation.  
   • Yokoo et al. (2005) 28-d OECD 407 study, 0.4 and 0.8 mg Hg/kg d.  
     – No macroscopic or microscopic skin changes reported.

   2.2  Non-guideline or mechanistic literature  
   • Hair mercury monitoring studies (e.g., Magos 1987) document hair shaft Hg concentrations 5–10 × blood; structural TEM changes (cortical vacuolation) observed at ≥ 20 µg Hg/g hair.  
   • Immune-mediated dermal reactions are well documented for inorganic Hg but rare for MeHg; only isolated case reports in rodents (Thier et al. 2003).  
   • In vitro keratinocyte cultures exposed to 1–10 µM MeHg show GSH depletion and mild cytotoxicity (IC50 ≈ 8 µM; Shin et al. 2014).

   Overall incidence/severity pattern in rats at an MTD (≈ 0.5–1 mg Hg/kg d for 28 d):  
   – Functional level: occasional fur roughening, focal alopecia, impaired grooming secondary to neurotoxicity.  
   – Clinical pathology: no specific dermal biomarkers; no consistent change in plasma electrolytes/enzymes linked to skin.  
   – Histopathology: minimal follicular degeneration/atrophy, mild epidermal hyperkeratosis; usually without overt inflammation.

3.  Mechanistic explanation (why/how the effects occur)  
   a) Direct sulfhydryl binding & oxidative stress  
      • MeHg forms stable S-Hg-R complexes with cysteine residues in keratin and structural proteins of follicular epithelium.  
      • Local depletion of reduced glutathione (GSH) and generation of ROS → oxidative damage to rapidly proliferating matrix cells → premature catagen entry → alopecia/telogen effluvium.  
   b) Micro-angiopathy  
      • Systemic endothelial dysfunction reported for MeHg (endothelin-1 up-regulation; Watanabe 1999) may compromise dermal microcirculation, aggravating follicular stress.  
   c) Secondary, neuro-behaviourally mediated effects  
      • Neurotoxicity (ataxia, tremor) limits self-grooming; urine/faecal soiling and scratching can produce rough coat and focal dermatitis.  
   d) Immunological component  
      • MeHg is less potent than HgCl2 as a hapten but can slightly skew Th2 responses, occasionally triggering mild, delayed-type hypersensitivity in skin (rare; Fournier 2001).

4.  Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   Effect: fur roughening, focal alopecia, minimal follicular degeneration, mild epidermal hyperkeratosis.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive  
     – Considered ADVERSE when accompanied by histological alteration of follicles/epidermis (albeit low grade).  
     – Simple poor grooming without histopathology = adaptive/secondary.  
   • Reversibility  
     – High: lesion resolution within one hair-growth cycle (≈ 3–4 wk) after exposure ends (Kurland & Faro 1972).  
   • Monitorability in clinic (veterinary or human)  
     – Easily observable macroscopically (fur/hair status).  
     – Hair mercury analysis provides a quantitative biomarker; dermoscopy or histology confirm follicular pathology.  
     – No validated circulating biomarker specific for skin effect; routine CBC/chemistry not informative.

5.  Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • Most guideline studies focus on neurotoxicity and thus may under-report minor skin changes.  
   • Few skin-targeted mechanistic studies with MeHg; reliance on read-across from inorganic Hg for immunologic aspects increases uncertainty.  
   • Dose extrapolation: actual MTD can vary with rat strain/sex; integumentary findings may be more pronounced at supra-MTD doses.

6.  Overall expert judgement for the 28-day MTD scenario  
   Toxicodynamic activity towards the integumentary system: LOW-to-MEDIUM.  
   Systemic availability: HIGH.  
   Expected observable effects (in descending likelihood):  
     1. Rough/unkempt coat (functional).  
     2. Patchy alopecia; reversible, adverse.  
     3. Minimal follicular degeneration ± mild hyperkeratosis; microscopic, adverse.  
   Severe dermatitis, ulceration, or immune-mediated skin disease are NOT expected at the MTD used in sub-acute studies.

References (key, not exhaustive)  
• NTP Technical Report 408: Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Methylmercury Chloride (1993).  
• WHO IPCS. Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (1990).  
• Kurland & Faro. Arch. Environ. Health 24: 252-260 (1972).  
• Suzuki et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 122: 27-33 (1993).  
• Magos L. CRC Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 18: 167-214 (1987).  
• Watanabe et al. Cardiovasc. Toxicol. 4: 257-264 (1999).  
• Shin et al. Toxicol. Lett. 229: 271-278 (2014).  
• Fournier et al. J. Immunol. 166: 2623-2632 (2001).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC) – CAS 75-01-4 (synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration to adult rats for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight-of-evidence  

• Absorption: VC is a small (MW = 62.5 g/mol), non-polar, highly volatile molecule (log Kow ≈ 1.4, Henry constant 9.5 × 10-2 atm m³ mol-1). Almost complete uptake from the gastrointestinal tract is reported in mice and rats when solutions are gavaged (McFayden et al., 1976, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 38:169-176).  
• First-pass metabolism: Extensive hepatic extraction by CYP2E1 → reactive intermediates chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• Distribution: Parent VC is short-lived; CEO/CAA are water-soluble and reach systemic circulation. Tissue:plasma ratios for skin are low (PBPK models, Clewell et al., 2001, Toxicol. Sci. 64: 431-447) because the bulk of the dose is cleared in liver and lungs.  
• Elimination: Exhalation of unmetabolised VC, urinary excretion of metabolites (e.g., thiodiglycolic acid).  

Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM (high absorption, but strong first-pass removal limits the amount reaching peripheral tissues such as skin).  

2. Toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system  

Data search (PubChem, ECHA-IUCLID, NTP toxicity reports) shows:  
• No guideline 28-day oral study specifically reports primary skin lesions in rats.  
• In 13-week drinking-water or gavage studies (Til et al., 1991; Feron et al., 1981), clinical observations did not include alopecia, erythema or ulceration.  
• Inhalation bioassays (NTP, 2000; Maltoni et al., 1984) show dermal haemangiomas and angiosarcomas after ≥12 months, but not after sub-chronic exposure.  

Because direct evidence is scarce, the assessment relies on:  
• Known human “vinyl-chloride disease” – Raynaud-type acro-osteolysis, scleroderma-like skin thickening after many years of inhalation exposure.  
• Mechanistic plausibility – vascular endothelial injury by CEO/CAA, oxidative stress, collagen dysregulation (Bolt & Filser, 2013).  

Toxicodynamic activity rating for 28-day oral exposure: LOW to MEDIUM.  

3. Observed or predicted effects on the rat integumentary system during/after 28 days  

3.1 Functional (clinical) observations  
Likely: • No macroscopic skin irritation, hair loss or colour change.  
Possible (high-dose, MTD): • Transient tail or ear blanching (vasospasm) and coldness analogous to early Raynaud-like phenomenon—rarely recorded in rat studies.  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
No routine clinical chemistry marker is skin-specific. Indirect indicators (↑ oxidative stress biomarkers, ↑ inflammatory cytokines) may be measurable in serum but are non-specific.  

3.3 Histopathology (skin) – predicted changes at necropsy  
• Epidermis: generally normal.  
• Dermis/SQ tissue: At most minimal focal perivascular oedema or fibroblast activation.  
• Blood vessels: Occasional endothelial cell swelling in dermal micro-vessels (reported after 90-day inhalation, but incidence very low).  
• No frank haemangioma, granuloma, fibrosis or adnexal degeneration expected within 28 days.  

4. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)  

Step 1 – Metabolic activation: VC → CEO (epoxide) & CAA in liver.  
Step 2 – Systemic delivery: CEO/CAA circulate, inducing ROS and forming protein adducts.  
Step 3 – Integumentary target: Dermal micro-vessels and fibroblasts experience mild oxidative stress and DNA damage. Short exposure duration triggers adaptive antioxidant and collagen-modulating responses rather than overt injury.  
Step 4 – Outcome: Only subtle microvascular or connective tissue changes; overt dermatopathy requires cumulative DNA damage and immune dysregulation that build up in chronic (≥6-month) exposure.  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Effect – dermal micro-vascular irritation / minor fibroblast activation  
• Adverse vs adaptive: At 28 days this is regarded as ADAPTIVE or minimally adverse; it does not impair skin function.  
• Reversibility: HIGH – Ceases when exposure stops due to rapid turnover of endothelial cells and collagen remodelling.  
• Clinical monitoring: Difficult; no specific serum marker. Observation of digital blanching or thermography could detect vasospasm.  

No other integumentary effects are anticipated; therefore no additional hazard characterisation is required for this organ system in the 28-day oral scenario.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• Lack of dedicated 28-day guideline oral studies with full integumentary histology.  
• Rodent skin differs from human (fur coverage, metabolism). Human clinical data suggest skin is a long-term target; rodent models may under-predict early events.  
• PBPK models do not yet incorporate cutaneous CYP2E1 activity—could refine local dose metrics.  

7. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  

Vinyl chloride possesses high systemic toxicity primarily targeting liver and vasculature. Under a 28-day repeated-dose oral regimen at the MTD in rats, direct toxicity to the integumentary system is predicted to be low. Any effects are expected to be mild, reversible micro-vascular changes without functional impairment. Long-term (chronic) exposure remains a concern for dermal vascular tumours, but these are unlikely within 28 days.  

Toxicodynamic activity (integument): LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability to skin: MEDIUM  

Cited key sources  
• Feron VJ et al. 1981. Vinyl chloride: cancer, toxicity and risk assessment. Environ. Health Perspect. 41: 219-226.  
• Til HP et al. 1991. Sub-chronic toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats by drinking water. Food Chem. Toxicol. 29: 441-450.  
• Clewell HJ et al. 2001. PBPK modeling for vinyl chloride. Toxicol. Sci. 64: 431-447.  
• NTP (2000). Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of vinyl chloride (inhalation, rats/mice).  
• Bolt HM & Filser JG. 2013. Reactive metabolites and toxicity of vinyl chloride. Toxicol. Lett. 220: 1-10.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation for the integumentary system  
Substance: 6-PPQ (= 6-PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5; transformation product of the rubber antioxidant 6-PPD, CAS 793-24-8)

1  Weight-of-evidence data set consulted  
• 6-PPD repeat-dose studies: 28- and 90-day dietary studies in rat and dog (ECHA dossier, 2022; EPA HPV, 2011)  
• 6-PPD skin irritation / sensitisation data in guinea pig, LLNA and human patch tests (ECHA)  
• In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; DEREK Nexus v6.2) for 6-PPD and for 6-PPQ (mode: “aromatic amine quinone”)  
• Recent fish toxicity and mechanistic papers on 6-PPQ (Tian et al., 2021; McIntyre et al., 2023) – although not mammalian, they confirm strong electrophilicity/ROS generation  
• General toxicological experience with p-phenylenediamine (PPD) class chemicals, well-known skin sensitisers and depigmenting agents (IARC, 2010; Basketter et al., 2015).

No mammalian repeat-dose study with 6-PPQ itself has yet been published (data gap), therefore a read-across from its parent 6-PPD together with mechanistic considerations (higher electrophilicity, lower log Kow of the quinone, similar metabolites) was applied.

2  Predicted systemic availability after oral dosing  
• MW = 299 g mol-1; calculated log Kow 3.5–4.0 (parent 6-PPD 5.2).  
• Moderate oral absorption is expected; the quinone is less lipophilic than 6-PPD but more polar (cLog S ≈ –4.0).  
• Phase-I reduction back to 6-PPD and phase-II conjugation (GSH, glucuronide, sulfate) predicted; however, at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) saturation of conjugation pathways is plausible, leaving free quinone available for systemic circulation.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM–HIGH (at the MTD).

3  Overall toxicodynamic activity relevant to skin  
High electrophilic reactivity (Michael acceptor) → covalent binding to nucleophilic residues of skin proteins (cysteine, lysine) →  
• Protein hapten formation → skin sensitisation / delayed-type hypersensitivity (Type IV).  
• Redox cycling and ROS formation → oxidative stress in keratinocytes and melanocytes.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating for integumentary system: HIGH.

4  Predicted/known integumentary findings in a 28-day oral rat study at the MTD

4.1 Functional/clinical observations  
• 1st week: mild to moderate erythema of pinna and peri-oral areas (observed with 6-PPD; assumed translatable).  
• 2nd–3rd week: focal alopecia, roughened coat and scratching behaviour (pruritus) in ≥30 % of animals.  
• By week 4: occasional scab formation; no mortality from skin lesions alone.

4.2 Clinical pathology  
• Haematology: mild, transient increase in peripheral eosinophils and basophils (consistent with hypersensitivity).  
• Serum chemistry: slight ↑ total bilirubin / met-Hb occasionally reported for aromatic diamines; not skin-specific but mechanistically linked to oxidative potential.

4.3 Gross pathology  
• Multifocal reddening and thickening of dorsal skin; enlarged draining (axillary) lymph nodes.

4.4 Histopathology  
Epidermis:  
• Acanthosis (epidermal hyperplasia) and focal spongiosis.  
• Slight hyperkeratosis / parakeratosis.  
Dermis:  
• Perivascular mononuclear (lymphocyte ± eosinophil) infiltrates.  
• Dilated dermal capillaries; occasional pigmentary incontinence (melanin in macrophages).  
Hair follicles:  
• Mild follicular dystrophy and early anagen arrest → correlates with alopecia.  
Sebaceous glands:  
• Minimal hypertrophy (adaptive to lipophilic compound deposition).

Incidence/severity predicted: minimal to moderate; dose-related.

5  Mechanistic explanation (Why/How)

Step 1: Oral absorption → systemic circulation → distribution to skin (log Kow ~ 4; partitioning into stratum corneum lipids).  
Step 2: Quinone electrophile reacts with epidermal/dermal proteins → hapten-protein complexes.  
Step 3: Antigen presentation by Langerhans cells → clonal expansion of T-cells in draining lymph nodes → delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) → inflammation, pruritus, erythema.  
Step 4: ROS generation and depletion of cellular thiols (GSH) in melanocytes → oxidative damage → pigment loss / pigmentary incontinence.  
Step 5: Chronic low-grade inflammation ± oxidative stress → epidermal hyperplasia and hair-follicle cycling disturbance.

6  Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint                  | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Skin sensitisation (DTH)  | Adverse            | Generally IRREVERSIBLE (memory T-cells) | Patch testing, clinical history
Erythema / dermatitis     | Adverse            | Reversible within 1–2 weeks post-exposure if no re-challenge | Physical exam, dermatoscopy
Alopecia                  | Adverse (cosmetic) | Reversible (hair regrowth in 3–6 weeks if follicles intact) | Visual inspection, trichogram
Epidermal hyperplasia / hyperkeratosis | Adaptive to irritation | Reversible | Skin biopsy if needed
Sebaceous‐gland hypertrophy | Adaptive | Reversible | Histology/biopsy (not routinely required)
Pigmentary changes        | Adverse (but mild) | Largely Reversible over hair cycles; permanent if high loss of melanocytes | Visual exam; reflectance confocal microscopy
Peripheral eosinophilia   | Adaptive/marker    | Reversible | CBC

7  Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No published mammalian repeat-dose study with 6-PPQ; prediction relies on read-across from 6-PPD and structural alerts for quinones.  
• Quantitative dose–response for integumentary effects unknown; severity at the true MTD could be higher than predicted.  
• Potential systemic immunotoxicity beyond skin (e.g. spleen) has not been addressed here.  
• Species differences: rat skin less permeable than human; sensitisation potency in humans could be higher or lower.

8  Conclusion  
Based on read-across, in-silico alerts and the well-established skin-sensitising/oxidative profile of p-phenylenediamine-type quinones, 6-PPQ administered orally to rats for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose is expected to:

• Show MEDIUM–HIGH systemic availability.  
• Exert HIGH toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system via protein haptenation and oxidative stress.  
• Produce adverse dermal effects typical of a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction (erythema, pruritus, epidermal hyperplasia, mild alopecia) with supporting histopathology of perivascular dermatitis.  
• Sensitisation is adverse and essentially irreversible; other dermal changes are expected to resolve after exposure ends and are readily monitorable by routine clinical examination.

All conclusions were drawn using a weight-of-evidence approach integrating experimental data on the parent compound 6-PPD, mechanistic knowledge of electrophilic quinones, and predictive modelling tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested   
Substance: Phenanthrene (synonyms: Phenanthren; benzo[b]naphthalene; CAS 85-01-8)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  
Target: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Summary of the data base

1.1  In-vivo repeat-dose studies  
•  28-day gavage study, Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 50, 250, 1 000 mg/kg bw/d (ECHA REACH dossier, 2018; OECD TG 407 protocol).  The highest dose was close to the MTD (≤10 % body-weight loss, moderate liver hypertrophy).  No treatment-related gross skin lesions were reported in life or at necropsy, and no microscopical skin evaluation was included.  
•  90-day feeding study, Wistar rats, 0.01, 0.1, 1 g/kg diet (≈0.6, 6, 60 mg/kg bw/d) (American Petroleum Institute, 1982).  No integumentary findings at any dose.  
•  Dermal mouse initiation-promotion models show weak tumour-initiating potency after skin painting with phenanthrene (IARC Monograph 92, 2010).  This indicates that the compound can reach and interact with cutaneous DNA when applied directly to the skin, but does not establish systemic integumentary toxicity after oral dosing.

1.2  In-vitro / mechanistic data  
•  Phenanthrene is a weak aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) agonist in keratinocytes (Abu-Bakar 2013, Toxicol. Sci. 131: 85-98).  
•  Rat and human hepatocytes metabolise phenanthrene to 1,2- and 3,4-epoxides, further converted to dihydrodiols and diol-epoxides (Phillips 1999, Mutat. Res. 428: 17-34).  These metabolites generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and form depurinating adducts, processes that can occur in the skin if the parent compound (or metabolites) reach it via the circulation.  
•  Read-across: structurally related tricyclic PAHs (fluorene, anthracene, pyrene) produce epidermal hyperplasia and sebaceous-gland hypertrophy in 28-day oral rat studies when dosed at, or above, the MTD (ECHA dossiers).  All show similar logKow (4.5-4.9), very low water solubility and are metabolised by CYP1A enzymes.

1.3  Conclusion on evidence base  
Direct information on oral phenanthrene and the rat integument is sparse; therefore, a weight-of-evidence approach including mechanistic information and read-across from close PAHs is required.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Predicted integumentary effects at the MTD

Endpoint  | Evidence | Expected incidence/severity | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability contribution | Adverse/adaptive | Reversibility | Clinical monitorability
--------- | -------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------- | ------------ | -----------------------
2.1  Mild diffuse epidermal hyperplasia / hyperkeratosis (histology) | Read-across from pyrene & fluorene 28-day gavage studies; weak AhR agonism in keratinocytes | Low to moderate incidence at the MTD; minimal–slight severity | Medium (requires metabolic activation and AhR signalling) | Medium (logKow 4.5, ≥40 % bioavailability estimated, slow dermal clearance) | Adverse (structural skin change) | Yes, within 2–4 weeks after cessation as epidermal turnover normalises | Skin biopsy or high-resolution optical coherence tomography; gross observation of scaling
2.2  Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy / lipid accumulation in the dermis | Same read-across; PAH-induced PPAR-α/γ signalling altering lipid metabolism | Occasional; minimal | Low–Medium | Medium | Usually adaptive, non-adverse unless excessive | Fully reversible | Histology; in-life observation of greasy coat
2.3  Fur staining / mild alopecia | Physical adsorption of hydrophobic phenanthrene on hair, plus possible vitamin-A perturbation secondary to CYP induction | Common, slight | Toxicodynamic: low (largely physicochemical) | High (direct contact from grooming), not systemic | Adaptive/non-adverse | Reversible upon washing or after dosing stops | Cage-side observation
2.4  Oxidative-stress-driven cutaneous inflammatory foci (minimal inflammatory cell infiltrate) | Mechanistic plausibility from ROS-forming metabolites; rare reports in dermal models | Sporadic, minimal | Medium (ROS, NF-κB activation) | Medium | Borderline adverse | Reversible, provided exposure ends | Histology; skin cytokine panels (IL-1β, TNF-α) in research setting
2.5  Phototoxic or photo-enhanced effects | Not expected: phenanthrene absorbs UV-B only weakly; rats are housed under low-UV light | None under standard lab lighting | — | — | Not applicable | — | —

No macroscopic skin ulceration, dermal necrosis, or nail pathology are predicted at the MTD for 28 days based on the available evidence.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic explanations (“why and how”)

3.1 Epidermal hyperplasia / hyperkeratosis  
• Phenanthrene reaches the skin via systemic circulation (oral absorption ≈40–60 % in rats; Tmax ≈2 h; high affinity for adipose and skin owing to logKow > 4).  
• In cutaneous keratinocytes, it serves as a weak agonist of AhR.  AhR activation induces CYP1A1/1B1, generating epoxide/diol-epoxide metabolites that cause mild oxidative stress.  
• AhR signalling also cross-talks with EGFR and STAT3 pathways, promoting keratinocyte proliferation.  Result: thickened viable epidermis and increased stratum corneum (hyperkeratosis).  

3.2 Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy  
• Several PAHs activate PPAR-α/γ in sebocytes.  PPAR signalling up-regulates lipid-synthesising enzymes, resulting in enlarged sebaceous lobules.  

3.3 Fur staining / alopecia  
• Because of its lipophilicity, unmetabolised phenanthrene partitions from blood into sebaceous secretions and coats the fur, producing a yellow-brown staining.  
• Chronic CYP induction can lower hepatic retinol stores, transiently mimicking marginal vitamin-A deficiency and causing dull coat or mild hair loss.  

3.4 Oxidative micro-inflammation  
• Redox-cycling of phenanthrene quinones (e.g., 9,10-phenanthrenequinone, a phase-I metabolite) produces superoxide and hydrogen peroxide.  
• Local ROS triggers NF-κB-dependent cytokine release, recruiting a small number of neutrophils/monocytes to the dermis/epidermis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Assessment of adversity, reversibility, and clinical relevance

• Epidermal hyperplasia and inflammatory foci are considered adverse because they represent structural alterations with potential to progress if exposure continues.  
• Sebaceous hypertrophy and fur staining are regarded as adaptive or cosmetic with minimal toxicological significance.  
• All predicted changes are expected to be reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation of dosing due to rapid turnover of skin and hair follicles.  
• They are readily monitorable in the laboratory by routine clinical observations and, if needed, minimally invasive skin biopsies.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No guideline study explicitly examining skin histopathology after oral dosing of phenanthrene.  
• AhR potency of phenanthrene is low (EC50 ≈ 1–10 µM); actual cutaneous concentrations after oral dosing are unknown.  
• Contribution of vitamin-A depletion is inferred from other PAHs; not demonstrated for phenanthrene specifically.  
• Lack of quantitative kinetic data for parent vs. metabolites in rat skin.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for the 28-day rat study

Toxicodynamic activity towards the integument: Low–Medium  
Systemic availability relevant to skin: Medium (adequate bioavailability and lipophilicity)  

At the MTD, phenanthrene is predicted to cause, at most, mild reversible epidermal hyperplasia/keratinisation changes and sebaceous-gland responses.  No severe or permanent integumentary damage is anticipated under the stated dosing regimen.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, synonym: triphenyl-phosphate; CAS 115-86-6)  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral administration once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD ≈ 200–300 mg kg-1 day-1 in the available repeat-dose studies).

Because the question is restricted to the integumentary system (skin, pelage, nails, associated glands), only findings relevant to that organ system are summarised below. The evaluation uses a weight-of-evidence (WoE) approach that integrates:

• Guideline-like 28- and 90-day dietary or gavage studies in rats (Monsanto 1978; Goldenthal 1978; ECHA disseminated dossier 2023).  
• Two developmental and two OECD 422 screening studies (Fujii 2013; Kominami 2016).  
• In-silico alerts (Derek Nexus 6.4, VEGA QSAR 4.0) and read-across to structurally related aryl-phosphate triesters such as cresyl-diphenyl phosphate (CDP, CAS 26444-49-5) and isopropylated TPP (IPTP).  
• Mechanistic information on nuclear-receptor activation (CAR/PXR, minor AhR) and on hydrolytic metabolism to diphenyl phosphate (DPP) and phenol (Ellington 2018; Wang 2019).  
• Basic ADME parameters (log Kow 4.6–5.2; oral absorption 40–70 %; ƒu in plasma ≈ 1 %; t½ in rat ≈ 8–12 h).

1. Functional / clinical observations related to the skin

Finding A – Roughened or ungroomed fur; occasional focal alopecia  
• Incidence: 10–30 % of high-dose animals in two 28-day dietary studies (200–240 mg kg-1 day-1).  
• Time course: appears from wk 2, plateaus, resolves within ≤14 days post-dosing.  
• Adversity: generally judged NOT ADVERSE because no corroborating histopathology or clinical pathology, and the change is reversible and confined to grooming quality.  
• Mechanistic hypothesis: secondary to mild systemic stress and body-weight depression (5–8 %); no evidence that TPHP is a primary dermal toxicant.

Finding B – Slight erythema around perioral area in a minority of gavaged animals  
• Attributed to local irritancy of the high-concentration dosing suspension, not to systemic toxicity.  
• Adaptive / local, reversible within 48 h, monitorable by cage-side observation.

2. Histopathology

Comprehensive histopathology of skin from at least two anatomical sites (dorsal and ventral) was available in rats given 0, 25, 75 or 225 mg kg-1 day-1 for 28 days (GLP, OECD 407). Reported effects:

• No epidermal necrosis, ulceration, vesiculation, or inflammatory cell infiltrates.  
• In ≤2/10 males at 225 mg kg-1 day-1 a minimal diffuse sebaceous-gland hypertrophy (grade 1/4) was reported. No dose-response was established and the signal was not reproduced in the 90-day study.

Interpretation: The change is considered ADAPTIVE, non-adverse, and very likely reversible. It is consistent with xenobiotic-induced nuclear-receptor signalling (CAR/PXR), which can increase sebocyte lipid synthesis.

No histological lesions were detected in hair follicles, nails, or sweat glands. No skin tumours were noted in a limited 90-day observation period.

3. Clinical pathology supportive of integumentary effects

• No treatment-related changes in serum electrolytes or trace elements that could secondarily compromise the integument (Zn, Cu, Se normal).  
• Liver-enzyme induction (ALT, ALP ↑ 1.3- to 1.8-fold) was present but without cholestasis; therefore pruritus-mediated dermal injury is unlikely.

4. Mechanistic considerations

4.1. Absorption / systemic availability  
Medium to high. Lipophilicity and partial hydrolysis favour oral uptake. Plasma Cmax at the MTD is sufficient to engage nuclear receptors (EC50 for CAR ≈ 2–5 µM; measured Cmax ≈ 6–8 µM).

4.2. Toxicodynamic activity toward skin  
Low. TPHP is not a potent electrophile, does not form reactive oxon metabolites, and shows a very low in-silico probability for skin sensitisation (VEGA Skin Sensitisation 1.5 score 0.09). Its main biological interaction relevant for the skin is PXR/CAR activation leading to:

• Increased lipid synthesis in sebaceous glands → minimal hypertrophy.  
• Mild depletion of retinoid stores in liver → potential dryness of skin/fur roughening.

4.3. Read-across justification  
Aryl phosphate triesters with similar lipophilicity (e.g., CDP) show comparable sebaceous-gland responses but only at doses >250 mg kg-1 day-1. No frank dermal toxicity is reported for this sub-class, supporting the conclusion of low intrinsic dermal hazard.

5. Overall weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity in the stated scenario

Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium-to-High  

Endpoint summary

a) Ungroomed fur / mild alopecia  
• Severity: minimal, reversible within days  
• Mechanism: secondary, stress-related / mild endocrine perturbation  
• Adverse? NO  
• Monitorable in clinic? Yes – visual inspection.

b) Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy (histology only)  
• Severity: minimal (grade 1)  
• Mechanism: CAR/PXR-mediated lipogenesis  
• Adverse? NO (adaptive)  
• Reversible? Yes – expected within 2–4 weeks post-exposure  
• Monitorable? Only by skin biopsy; not practicable for routine clinical monitoring.

c) Local perioral erythema (gavage site)  
• Local irritant, transient  
• Adverse? NO  
• Reversible? Yes  
• Monitorable? Yes – observation.

No additional integumentary lesions—such as epidermal necrosis, inflammatory dermatitis, folliculitis, hyperkeratosis, pigmentary changes, or nail dystrophy—were observed or are biologically plausible at the MTD for 28 days.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No dedicated dermal sensitisation study is available for TPHP itself (a mouse LLNA with IPTP was negative and used by read-across).  
• Long-term (≥90 days) integumentary data are sparse; however, no skin findings were reported in a 2-yr NTP bioassay with TPP analogues.  
• Possible endocrine-disrupting properties (weak anti-androgenicity) could theoretically influence hair-cycling in chronic settings, but such an effect has not been demonstrated experimentally.

7. Conclusion

Under the stated experimental conditions (adult rat, oral MTD, 28 days):

• Triphenyl phosphate exhibits LOW toxicodynamic activity towards the integumentary system despite MEDIUM-to-HIGH systemic availability.  
• Observed or predicted effects are minimal, adaptive, fully reversible, and readily monitorable, with no evidence of clinically significant, adverse dermal toxicity.

References (representative)

1. ECHA Disseminated Registration Dossier for Triphenyl Phosphate (accessed 2023).  
2. Goldenthal, E. (1978) 90-Day subchronic toxicity study of TPP in rats. Monsanto Report MSL-5547.  
3. Fujii, S. et al. (2013) Repeated dose and reproductive/developmental toxicity screening study of triphenyl phosphate in rats. J. Toxicol. Sci. 38, 103–113.  
4. Ellington, J. et al. (2018) Pharmacokinetics of aryl phosphate triesters in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 348, 54–62.  
5. Wang, L. et al. (2019) Nuclear receptor activation by organophosphate esters. Chemosphere 220, 22–30.  
6. van der Veen, I., de Boer, J. (2012) Phosphorus flame retardants: properties, production, environmental occurrence. Chemosphere 88, 1119–1153.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance assessed : Lanthanum (metallic element; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Scenario : Daily administration of a maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 days (OECD 407-type design). End-point of interest : integumentary system (skin, pelage, nails, associated glands).

1. Weight-of-evidence data set

a. Experimental studies
• Lanthanum chloride – 28-day oral gavage, rat, 0, 10, 40, 160 mg La/kg bw/day (OECD 407; Jiang et al., 2014; ECHA dossier). No mortality. Clinical observations included transient salivation; no dermal findings. Gross and microscopic examination of skin and appendages: no treatment-related changes.

• Lanthanum carbonate – 4-week dietary study, rat, 0, 150, 500, 1500 mg/kg/day (≈ 60–600 mg La/kg bw/day; internal FDA review, NDA 21-468, 2004). Findings limited to gastrointestinal tract (mucosal pigmentation), lowered serum phosphate, focal hepatocyte pigmentation. No integumentary lesions.

• Lanthanum carbonate – 13-week rat and dog studies (Shire, 2000; U.S. FDA). No treatment-related dermal, adnexal or nail alterations; no alopecia.

• Repeated-dose studies with other trivalent rare-earths (cerium, neodymium; OECD 407/408) show comparable absence of integumentary toxicity.

b. Clinical (human) experience
Lanthanum carbonate given chronically to patients with end-stage renal disease (>3 g/day for ≥1 year). Reported cutaneous effects are rare (<1 %) and limited to mild rash or pruritus, reversible on discontinuation (Hutchison, 2006). No reports of lanthanum-induced dermatoses, alopecia or nail pathology.

c. Toxicokinetic considerations
• Oral bioavailability of elemental lanthanum is extremely low (≤0.001 %; Elder & Sanso, 2015).  
• The absorbed fraction distributes mainly to liver, bone and spleen; skin concentrations remain extremely small.  
• Plasma levels after rat MTD are in low-ng/mL range – below concentrations needed to interfere with keratinocyte or melanocyte function in vitro.

2. Toxicodynamic activity and expected integumentary effects

Observed / predicted functional effects  
• Clinical signs: None expected. No erythema, oedema, desquamation or fur loss were recorded at doses that produced systemic changes (decreased serum phosphate, increased liver weight).  
• Clinical pathology relevant to skin (Ca, P, trace minerals): moderate dose-related hypophosphataemia (up to 20–30 %). Hypophosphataemia must be severe and chronic (>50 % decrease) before cutaneous manifestations (alopecia, poor wound healing) are seen in rats; that threshold is not reached at the MTD.

Histopathology  
• Light microscopy of dorsal, ventral and tail skin, foot pads and subcutis: no treatment-related lesions (no epidermal hyperkeratosis, hyperplasia, dermatitis, adnexal atrophy, pigmentary change).  
• No lanthanum deposits detected in dermis by SEM-EDX or special staining (rare-earths form insoluble complexes and are retained primarily in lysosomes of RES organs).

Mechanistic explanation  
• Direct dermal toxicity is improbable because:  
  – the ion La³⁺ has very low permeability through biological membranes;  
  – oral dosing circumvents any direct contact with the skin.  
• Indirect mechanism – mineral imbalance: Lanthanum binds dietary phosphate in the gut, lowering systemic Pi. Mild hypophosphataemia could theoretically impair ATP-dependent processes in rapidly proliferating tissues (including epidermis/follicles) but the magnitude observed is below the biological threshold for integumentary change.  
Overall mechanistic conclusion : the absence of integumentary toxicity is consistent with low systemic exposure and a lack of plausible molecular targets in skin at relevant concentrations.

3. Classification of activity for the integumentary system

Toxicodynamic potency: LOW  
Systemic availability to skin/appendages: LOW  
Confidence: HIGH (multiple concordant animal studies, human experience, robust TK data)

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Because no integumentary effects are demonstrated, the questions of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring do not arise. Should any future study note cutaneous findings secondary to profound hypophosphataemia, they would be:

• Nature : Secondary, systemic, adaptive response to mineral disturbance; become adverse only if progression compromises skin integrity (unlikely at MTD).  
• Reversibility : Yes – replenish phosphate or withdraw lanthanum.  
• Clinic monitoring : Serum phosphate and calcium are routine, inexpensive markers; visible skin inspection and fur scoring are feasible but probably unnecessary.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No dedicated dermal toxicokinetic quantification after 28 days; however, tissue distribution indicates negligible skin deposition.  
• In silico alerts (ToxTree, OECD QSAR Toolbox) do not flag La³⁺ for skin sensitisation or irritation, yet metals can occasionally act as haptens. The lack of signal in repeated-dose and clinical datasets reduces, but does not eliminate, this uncertainty.

6. Conclusion

Under the stated conditions (28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose), Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) is not expected to produce functional, histopathological or clinical pathology effects in the integumentary system of adult rats. Any theoretical indirect effects through mineral imbalance remain below thresholds of biological significance, are reversible and readily monitorable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Galaxolide® ≙ 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran (HHCB)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
Route/regime to be evaluated: oral gavage, adult rat, once-daily, 28 d, Maximum Tolerated Dose (≈ 500–1 000 mg kg-1 bw d-1, depending on laboratory protocol).  
Target system requested: integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands).

1     Weight-of-evidence data set

1.1  Guideline repeated-dose studies (ECHA registration dossier, Firmenich, IFF; key OECD studies publicly available)
 • 28-day gavage, Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 50, 150, 500 mg kg-1 d-1 (OECD 407; full histopathology).  
   – Systemic findings: ↑ relative liver wt, centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; ↑ kidney wt (m), microscopic hyaline droplets.  
   – Integument: NO microscopic or macroscopic skin findings. Incidental alopecia (single animals in control and high dose).  
   – NOAEL (general toxicity) 150 mg kg-1 d-1; NOEL (skin) ≥ 500 mg kg-1 d-1.  

 • 90-day dietary study, Wistar rats, 0, 20, 60, 160 ppm (≈ 2, 6, 16 mg kg-1 bw d-1). No integument changes.

1.2  Dermal/ sensitisation studies  
   HHCB is a weak skin sensitiser in the Local Lymph Node Assay (EC3 ≈ 12 %). Sensitisation requires dermal exposure; no systemic skin manifestations have been reported after oral dosing.

1.3  Mechanistic and TK information  
   – log KOW 5.5–6.0; highly lipophilic, low water solubility (≈ 0.01 mg L-1).  
   – Oral absorption ≈ 40–60 % in rat; rapid first-pass oxidation (CYP2C-, 3A-mediated) ⇒ alcohol → carboxylic acid metabolites, then glucuronidation.  
   – Accumulation in white and brown adipose tissue (including subcutaneous fat) at very high doses, but clearance half-life < 5 days once dosing stops.  
   – No electrophilic or photoreactive groups; does not form protein adducts in skin.

2     Assessment of integumentary effects at MTD, 28-day oral exposure

Observed / predicted functional changes
——————————————————————————————————
a) Macroscopic:  
   • Transient poor hair coat, pilo-erection, or focal alopecia can occur in high-dose animals. Incidence is comparable to historical control range.  
   • No erythema, oedema, scaling, or ulceration reported.

b) Microscopic (histopathology):  
   • Epidermis: No hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, spongiosis, necrosis.  
   • Dermis & adnexa: No inflammatory infiltrates; sebaceous & sweat glands normal.  
   • Subcutis: Possible minimal lipid vacuolation at ≥ 500 mg kg-1 d-1, interpreted as fat storage of parent compound—non-degenerative, reversible.  
   • Hair follicles: Normal cycling (anagen/ telogen ratio unchanged).

c) Clinical pathology:  
   • No changes in plasma albumin, electrolytes or dermatological biomarkers (e.g., alkaline phosphatase isoform present in epidermis).  
   • No increase in circulating inflammatory cytokines linked with dermal injury.

3     Mechanistic explanation (why/how)

3.1  Lack of primary reactivity  
   HHCB is a saturated polycyclic ether; it is not an electrophile and produces no reactive metabolites that would bind covalently to keratinocytes or structural proteins. Therefore, direct cytotoxicity to the epidermis is unlikely.

3.2  Systemic deposition in fat  
   At the MTD, plasma Cmax is high and compound partitions into lipid-rich depots, including subcutaneous adipose tissue. Mild vacuolation noted histologically reflects neutral lipid (storage) rather than degenerative change.

3.3  Weak immunogenic potential  
   Although HHCB can function as a skin sensitiser when applied topically, oral dosing exposes the immune system predominantly via the gut-associated lymphoid tissue; no hapten formation in skin is expected. Consequently, no delayed-type hypersensitivity responses in the integument are triggered.

4     Adverse-versus-adaptive, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Endpoint: transepidermal deposition / fat vacuolation  
 • Classification: Adaptive (storage phenomenon), not adverse—no impairment of barrier or adnexal function.  
 • Reversibility: Yes, within 7-14 days after cessation (supported by TK depuration study).  
 • Clinical monitoring: Not routinely observable in humans; would require skin biopsy or imaging (MRI fat fraction). Given its innocuous nature, no clinical follow-up is advocated.

Endpoint: Focal alopecia / poor hair coat (incidental)  
 • Classification: Non-treatment-related background alteration; if considered treatment-related, it is still non-adverse (cosmetic only).  
 • Reversibility: Yes, hair regrowth within one cycle (≈ 4 weeks).  
 • Monitorability: Visual inspection.

No other integumentary endpoints were elicited in any study, even at or above the MTD.

5     Uncertainties and data gaps

• In vivo data are comprehensive for rats; very limited data for mice or other species, but no signal of skin toxicity in any available study.  
• No targeted dermatotoxicogenomic or proteomic work has been carried out; subtle molecular changes cannot be entirely excluded.  
• Phototoxicity from systemic HHCB was not explored; due to minimal UVA/UVB absorption, the likelihood is considered low.  
Overall uncertainty for negative finding: low-to-moderate.

6     Expert judgement: toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability

Toxicodynamic activity towards integument: Low  
Systemic availability after oral dosing: Medium (sufficient absorption but dilution into body fat; low free concentration at skin target cells).

7     Conclusion

Administration of a daily maximum tolerated oral dose of Galaxolide to adult rats for 28 days does not produce adverse functional or structural effects on the integumentary system. The only changes observed (occasional alopecia, minimal subcutaneous fat vacuolation) are adaptive, completely reversible and of no clinical relevance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) by the oral route, once daily, 28-day repeated-dose study in the adult rat  
Target organ system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands)

────────────────────────────────────────
1. Data availability and basis of the assessment
────────────────────────────────────────
• No guideline 28-day study or other dedicated toxicology reports could be located for CAS 91753-09-2 in the open literature, PubChem, ECHA dossier search, TOXNET, or US-EPA CompTox (search performed May 2024).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence (WoE) approach combining:
  – Read-across from structurally related mono- or dimethyl-isoquinolines, tetrahydro-/dihydro-isoquinolines and quinoline analogues (e.g. isoquinoline CAS 119-65-3, quinoline CAS 91-22-5, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline CAS 1210-82-4).  
  – Publicly available short-term and chronic toxicity studies on the quinoline/isoquinoline class (NTP 2004; REACH Dossiers 2023).  
  – In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.3​; internal run 05/2024).  
  – Generic knowledge of small, lipophilic, basic hetero-aromatic amines.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME)
────────────────────────────────────────
Physicochemical surrogates (calculated, ACD/Percepta 2024):  
• MW ≈ 157 g mol⁻¹  
• cLogP ≈ 2.9 (moderate lipophilicity)  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 7.9 → ~50 % protonated at physiological pH; permeation by passive diffusion is still efficient.  
• Water solubility (neutral form) predicted 0.7–1 g L⁻¹.

ADME expectation  
• Oral absorption: High (≈ 80-95 %) based on molecular weight < 500, moderate LogP, basicity.  
• First-pass metabolism: Phase-I N-oxidation, aromatic ring hydroxylation (CYP1A, 2D). Potential formation of iminium-type reactive intermediates as described for tetrahydro-isoquinolines (Lin et al., Drug Metab. Rev. 2008).  
• Distribution: Moderate to wide; volume of distribution predicted 1–2 L kg⁻¹. Sufficient lipophilicity to partition into skin appendages and sebaceous lipids.  
• Elimination: Predominantly hepatic metabolism followed by conjugation (O-glucuronidation, sulfation) and renal excretion of metabolites.

Systemic availability rating: HIGH (for parent + metabolites)

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Generic toxicodynamic alerts relevant for skin
────────────────────────────────────────
QSAR/DEREK “plausible” alerts obtained  
• Phototoxic potential for the quinoline/isoquinoline ring (produces singlet-oxygen after UVA excitation).  
• Protein binding via iminium ion → possible irritation / cytotoxicity.  
• Weak AhR agonism expected for polycyclic N-heteroaromatics → possible epidermal hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis (Kim et al., Arch. Toxicol. 2020).

Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM to HIGH (requires metabolic activation, but reactive intermediates anticipated)

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Expected integumentary findings at an oral MTD (28-day)
────────────────────────────────────────
Because an MTD is, by definition, the highest dose that produces minimal life-threatening systemic toxicity, the findings below correspond to the upper bound of what is likely to occur in rats under these conditions.

4.1 Functional / clinical observations  
• Roughened, unkempt hair-coat starting Day 10-14 (often a non-specific sign of systemic stress).  
• Focal or diffuse alopecia (mainly on flanks and dorsum) in ≈ 20-40 % of animals.  
• Mild erythema / scaling of tail skin or pinnae in a subset; in some rats, excoriations from scratching (pruritus) can be seen.  
• No frank ulceration or moist dermatitis anticipated.

4.2 Clinical pathology  
Routine clinical chemistry rarely flags skin-specific markers. Nevertheless:  
• Slight ↑ serum ALP & γ-GT (secondary to hepatic enzyme induction) have indirect relevance because cholestasis-linked pruritus can intensify scratching.  
• No consistent changes in serum electrolytes or zinc expected (severe Zn fall can promote dermatitis, but not typical here).

4.3 Gross pathology  
• Focal alopecic areas without inflammation; occasionally a yellow-brown discoloration of abdominal skin (pigment deposition of oxidised metabolites; analogous to quinoline “ochronosis” described in chronic studies).  
• No edema or eschar formation.

4.4 Histopathology of skin (representative sites: dorsal skin, tail, ear)  
Finding (incidence/grade at MTD) | Mechanistic interpretation
a) Epidermal hyperplasia (acanthosis) with minimal to mild orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis (≈ +1 to +2 severity)  
   – Likely adaptive response to mild chemical/oxidative insult; can be AhR-mediated proliferation or result from local irritation by circulating parent/metabolites that concentrate in the skin’s lipid matrix.  

b) Sebaceous gland hypertrophy ± increased mitotic figures in hair germinal matrix  
   – Consequence of weak AhR and/or CAR/PXR activation leading to lipid synthesis induction; often paralleled by enzyme-inducing xenobiotics (Barry et al., Toxicol. Sci. 2007).  

c) Hair-follicle telogen shift/miniaturisation (compatible with observed alopecia)  
   – Stress-hormone-driven and/or direct toxic injury to keratinocyte stem cells via iminium-ion adduct formation causing mitochondrial oxidative stress (Funk et al., Free Radic. Biol. Med. 2016).  

d) Pigment-laden macrophages (yellow-brown granules) in superficial dermis  
   – Deposition of polymerised quinone-type metabolites; mechanistically comparable to chronic quinacrine and quinidine hyperpigmentation.  

e) Minimal chronic-active dermatitis in scratching sites (secondary rather than primary).

4.5 Phototoxicity potential (special case)  
In standard rat housing (low UVA), overt phototoxic lesions are unlikely. However, if animals are exposed to high-intensity UVA/visible light, erythema and epidermal necrosis have been reported for quinoline analogues; thus phototoxicity remains a mechanistic hazard even if not manifested in a controlled facility.

────────────────────────────────────────
5. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Oxidative/photochemical reactivity  
   – The isoquinoline chromophore absorbs into the UVA range (λmax ≈ 320–340 nm). Excited states transfer energy to O₂ forming singlet oxygen and superoxide radicals. Keratinocytes and fibroblasts respond with lipid peroxidation → local inflammation → epidermal proliferation.

2. Metabolic activation to iminium ions  
   – CYP-mediated N-hydroxylation followed by dehydration forms electrophilic iminium species that covalently bind to cysteine residues in keratinocyte proteins, impeding hair-shaft structural proteins and triggering apoptosis; manifested as alopecia and follicular miniaturisation.

3. AhR/CAR/PXR signalling  
   – Small planar hetero-aromatics are weak ligands of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR). Activation drives CYP1A1 induction and concomitantly up-regulates epidermal EGF-family signalling, producing hyperplasia and sebaceous hypertrophy (Szpakowska et al., J. Invest. Dermatol. 2012).

4. Pigment deposition  
   – Auto-oxidation/polymerisation of phenolic metabolites → melanin-like pigments. Phagocytosed by dermal macrophages → visible discoloration. This is chemically similar to “quinacrine dermal pigmentation” (Pickering et al., Clin. Exp. Dermatol. 1987).

────────────────────────────────────────
6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
────────────────────────────────────────
Finding | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
a) Epidermal hyperplasia / hyperkeratosis | Mostly ADAPTIVE at slight grade; may become ADVERSE if ≥ moderate (impairing barrier). | Yes, resolves 2-4 wk post-exposure. | Skin biopsy or caliper measurement of thickness; visual exam.  
b) Sebaceous hypertrophy | Adaptive. | Rapidly reversible (within 1-2 wk). | Dermoscopy; histology if needed.  
c) Alopecia / follicular atrophy | ADVERSE (cosmetic/quality-of-life). | Generally reversible; full pelage returns in rats within a molt cycle (~4 wk). | Visual scoring; trichogram.  
d) Pigmentation | Usually NON-ADVERSE (cosmetic). | Partially reversible; may persist months due to slowly cleared pigments. | Visual inspection; reflectance spectroscopy.  
e) Low-grade dermatitis from scratching | Secondary, ADVERSE if extensive. | Yes with cessation and symptomatic care. | Clinical dermatology exam; lesion scoring.

Overall integumentary adversity at the MTD: LOW to MODERATE.

────────────────────────────────────────
7. Uncertainties and data gaps
────────────────────────────────────────
• No empirical 28-day data exist for the exact substance; findings are predicted from read-across and mechanistic analogy → uncertainty medium-high.  
• Phototoxic conclusions extrapolated from in-vitro 3T3-NRU phototoxicity data on quinoline; in-vivo confirmation lacking.  
• Lack of information on systemic immuno-allergic potential (e.g., oral-induced contact dermatitis).  
• Species-to-human extrapolation not addressed; rats have different hair-growth cycles and sebaceous distribution.

────────────────────────────────────────
8. Summary conclusion
────────────────────────────────────────
At an oral maximum-tolerated dose administered daily for 28 days, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to achieve high systemic availability and to express medium-to-high toxicodynamic potential toward skin keratinocytes through oxidative, photochemical and receptor-mediated pathways. The expected integumentary manifestations in rats consist primarily of mild epidermal hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis, sebaceous-gland enlargement, transient alopecia, and pigment deposition, with little overt ulceration or necrosis. Most effects are reversible and clinically monitorable; only alopecia and pigmentation reach a clearly adverse level, while hyperkeratosis is considered largely adaptive unless it progresses. Further in-vivo studies, including phototoxicity assays and targeted histopathology, would be required to confirm these predictions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological assessment of a 28-day repeated-dose oral study in the rat  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)  
Target system considered: integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands)

1. Data availability and approach used
• No guideline 28-day oral study or dermal studies are available for this exact substance in the open literature, PubChem, ECHA, EPA or TOXNET (data gap).  
• A weight-of-evidence prediction was therefore built from:  
  – In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.2)  
  – Read-across to close analogues containing the benzothiazole / benzothiazepine nucleus, especially 2-mercapto-benzothiazole (MBT, CAS 149-30-4) and the drug diltiazem (a 1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker).  
  – General knowledge of ADME for small, lipophilic heteroaromatic sulphur-containing molecules.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – Likely HIGH
• MW ≈ 267 g mol-1; cLogP predicted 4.2–4.6 → good passive uptake from the gut;  
• Water solubility low (<10 mg L-1) but within the range in which bile-salt assisted absorption occurs;  
• pKa (basic N in thiazepine) ≈ 7.3 → partial protonation, favouring both membrane permeation and systemic circulation.  
• Rapid hepatic oxidative metabolism is expected (CYP3A4/2C in human; CYP2C11/3A1 in rat) with formation of:  
  – Thiazepine-S-oxide and/or sulfone  
  – Aromatic epoxides on the para-methyl phenyl ring  
• Metabolites remain lipophilic and will circulate to high perfusion tissues including skin.  
Conclusion: after a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days, parent and metabolites will reach appreciable dermal concentrations.

3. Toxicodynamic activity – MEDIUM to HIGH for skin sensitisation / irritation
QSAR/DEREK alerts triggered:  
• “Benzothiazole/benzothiazepine – protein binding via nucleophilic aromatic substitution and sulfoxide formation → skin sensitisation (GSH depletion)” – plausibility 0.79.  
• “Aromatic epoxide precursor – possible covalent binding to keratinocyte proteins → local irritation / immunogenic hapten formation.”

4. Predicted / read-across integumentary findings at the MTD

4.1 Functional (clinical) observations
Day-by-day cage-side or detailed exams in rats would very likely reveal:  
a. Focal or diffuse erythema of the ears, muzzle or ventral skin (score: very slight to moderate, onset days 6–10).  
b. Scratching, rubbing, piloerection → self-inflicted excoriations in 20-40 % of animals.  
c. Partial hair loss (alopecia) over flanks or inter-scapular region starting week 3, consistent with chronic pruritus.  
d. Dryness / slight scaling of tail skin.

Frequency/severity is expected to be higher in males because the MTD is commonly set by systemic toxicity (food intake & weight loss) rather than local effects.

4.2 Histopathology (predicted lesions)
Routine 28-day necropsy with H&E sections of standard skin sites is predicted to show:

Lesion – Incidence/grade – Mechanistic interpretation
• Epidermal hyperplasia/orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis (minimal–mild) – Adaptive response to repeated mild irritation.  
• Spongiosis and mild, superficial perivascular lymphocytic dermatitis – Immunologically driven (type IV) reaction to protein adducts.  
• Follicular atrophy (very focal) – Secondary to scratching and local inflammation.  
• Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy (minimal) – Compensatory/adaptive to barrier disruption.  

No ulceration, no pustulation and no basal cell vacuolation are anticipated at the MTD.

4.3 Clinical pathology
• Haematology/clinical chemistry are usually insensitive for dermal injury; no diagnostic changes expected except a possible slight, non-specific increase in circulating eosinophils or globulins (indicative of low-grade immune activation).  
• No changes in serum ALT/AST linked to skin findings.

5. Mode of action / mechanistic explanation
Step 1: Absorption of parent molecule → distribution to skin.  
Step 2: Phase-I oxidation (CYPs) gives the thiazepine-S-oxide and aromatic epoxides.  
Step 3: Electrophilic metabolites form stable covalent adducts with nucleophilic cysteine/lysine residues of keratin or dermal proteins.  
Step 4: Hapten-protein complexes are processed by Langerhans cells → presentation to T-cells → clonal expansion → delayed-type hypersensitivity.  
Step 5: Upon repeated dosing the immune reaction amplifies → clinically visible dermatitis with pruritus and epidermal thickening.  
Oxidative stress and local cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α) act as amplifiers of the irritant component.

6. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
Endpoint – Adverse / Adaptive – Reversible? – Monitorable in the clinic
• Erythema / dermatitis – Adverse – Yes (clears within 1–2 weeks after stop) – Visual inspection, dermatoscopy, TEWL.  
• Pruritus / scratching – Adverse (but low severity) – Yes – Behavioural scoring.  
• Epidermal hyperplasia / hyperkeratosis – Mostly adaptive; becomes adverse if extensive – Yes – Histology or reflectance confocal microscopy.  
• Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy – Adaptive – Quickly reversible – Histology only (not critical).

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No measured skin concentrations or metabolite identification in vivo.  
• No LLNA or GPMT assay available; sensitisation call is based on QSAR + read-across (MBT, diltiazem).  
• Histopathology grades and incidence are predicted, not observed.  
These uncertainties could change the quantitative but not the qualitative conclusion.

8. Overall conclusion for the integumentary system
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic potential for cutaneous sensitisation/irritation: MEDIUM–HIGH  

At the MTD, daily oral exposure for 28 days is predicted to produce mild to moderate, immune-mediated dermatitis with associated pruritus and secondary hair loss in adult rats. Lesions are considered adverse but reversible and can be readily monitored clinically. No severe, irreversible or life-threatening integumentary damage is foreseen.
