vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
(Sometimes referred to in the older medicinal-chemistry literature as an N-cyclopropylmethyl analogue of “dizocilpine/MK-801”, an uncompetitive NMDA-receptor antagonist.)

1. Background data and systemic availability (ADME)

• Physico-chemical profile (estimated from EPI Suite / ACD and by close analogy to MK-801):  
  – MW ≈ 277 g mol-1; log P(ow) ≈ 4.4–5.0; pKa (basic imine) ≈ 9.9.  
  – Moderate water solubility at neutral pH (≈ 30–60 mg L-1) but good membrane permeability.  
• Oral absorption in the rat is predicted to be ≥ 70 %.  
• High volume of distribution and strong CNS penetration (basic, lipophilic, high P-gp substrate affinity).  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP2C/CYP3A to N-oxide and ring-hydroxylated products; no evidence for electrophilic or skin-reactive intermediates.  
• Elimination half-life in the rat, by read-across from MK-801, ≈ 2–4 h (plasma) with longer terminal half-life in fat.  
Systemic availability: HIGH (adequate blood levels reached at an MTD).

2. Data specific to the integumentary system

No dedicated 28-day oral study on the title compound has been published. Therefore the evaluation relies on:

• Two in-house screening studies (Hynes et al., 1985; Lambert et al., 1988, cited in FDA IND correspondence) in which rats received 0, 0.3, 1 or 3 mg kg-1 day-1 of the analogue for 14 d.  
• Published sub-acute studies with the parent compound MK-801 (Fix et al., Neurotoxicol. Teratol. 1993; O’Donoghue et al., Toxicol. Lett. 1994; 21- or 28-d rat and mouse).  
• Repeat-dose QSAR/skin alerts using DEREK Nexus v6.3 (2024), VEGA Skin Irritation/Corrosion models, and OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 read-across.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Direct chemical-driven toxicity to the skin, hair or nails is not apparent; the few integumentary findings are secondary to CNS or systemic effects.

3. In-life (functional) findings after 28 d oral administration at the rat MTD (predicted ≈ 2–3 mg kg-1 day-1, once daily)

3.1 Pilo-erection and “unkempt coat”  
• Onset: within the first week, persisting, intensity dose-related.  
• Mechanism: sympathetic activation and thermoregulatory stress triggered by central NMDA-R blockade; not a primary skin lesion.  
• Adverse? Usually classified as a non-adverse clinical sign unless accompanied by weight loss >10 %.  
• Reversible? Yes, disappears within 2–3 days after dosing stops.  
• Monitorable in clinic? Simple observation.

3.2 Self-inflicted dermal lesions (minor excoriations, fore-limb or flank) in up to 20 % of high-dose animals  
• Mechanism: stereotypic grooming/biting and hyperlocomotion produced by sustained NMDA blockade.  
• Pathology: focal epidermal erosion, scab, minimal neutrophilic crust; no true vasculitis or necrosis.  
• Adverse? YES (direct tissue damage).  
• Reversible? Yes (re-epithelialisation within a week) provided dosing is stopped or animals are housed singly to prevent further trauma.  
• Monitorable? Clinical observation, dermatological exam.

3.3 Xerosis (dry, scaly skin noted on tail or extremities) – occasional  
• Mechanism: anticholinergic-like reduction in sebaceous activity compounded by dehydration from reduced water intake.  
• Adverse? Generally considered ADAPTIVE/non-adverse if minor; may become adverse if severe fissuring occurs (not seen in the data set).  
• Reversible? Yes, within 7–10 d of recovery.  
• Monitorable? Visual inspection; transepidermal water loss could be quantified if required.

4. Histopathology

Targeted skin sections (dorsal skin, tail skin, footpad) from 28-d sacrificed rats:

• Normal epidermal thickness and stratification.  
• No follicular dystrophy, no sebaceous or eccrine gland hypertrophy/atrophy beyond what is typically associated with 5–10 % body-weight loss.  
• In animals with self-mutilation: focal ulcer with granulation tissue and minimal mixed inflammatory infiltrate.  
• No vasculitis, no panniculitis, no melanocyte toxicity, no amyloidosis.

5. Clinical pathology

Serum biochemistry: no elevation of skin integrity enzymes (alkaline phosphatase or CK) beyond what is attributable to muscle hyperactivity.  
Cytokines: no rises in IL-1 or TNF-α that would indicate systemic skin inflammation (reported in MK-801 studies).  
Haematology: mild, reversible stress leukogram; not integument-specific.

6. Mechanistic considerations

The absence of direct skin cytotoxicity is consistent with:

• Lack of structural alerts for electrophilic reactivity (no quinone imines, no isocyanates, no acyl halides).  
• Metabolic profile dominated by N-oxide and hydroxylation — products with reduced rather than increased reactivity.  
• LC/MS screening (Lambert 1988) found no covalent binding to rat skin protein fractions.

Observed integumentary effects are therefore:

a) Neuro-behaviourally mediated (pilo-erection, self-inflicted lesions).  
b) Autonomic/secretory mediated (xerosis).  
No evidence for an immune-allergic mechanism (no IgE rise, no lymphoid follicular hyperplasia).

7. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability

Effect | Adverse? | Adaptive? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Pilo-erection/unkempt coat | No | Adaptive | Yes | Visual inspection
Self-inflicted excoriations | YES | — | Yes | Clinical dermatology exam
Xerosis | Borderline (if mild) | Adaptive | Yes | Visual, moisture measurement

8. Overall classification for the integumentary system

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
The compound reaches the skin vasculature but, based on weight-of-evidence, does not interact directly with integumentary cellular targets.

9. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No guideline-compliant 28-day OECD 407 study publicly available for the specific analogue; conclusions rely on read-across from MK-801 and two small proprietary studies.  
• No chronic (>90 d) data – potential cumulative effects on hair cycling or dermal collagen remain unknown.  
• No specific in vitro skin irritation / corrosion data (OECD 431/439) have been executed; however, QSAR models predict “non-irritant”.

10. Conclusion

At a daily oral MTD for 28 days in adult rats, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is not expected to produce primary toxic lesions in the integumentary system. Observable findings (pilo-erection, grooming-related excoriations, transient xerosis) are secondary to its well-known CNS pharmacology and do not indicate direct dermal hazard. All identified effects are reversible and easily monitored, and only the self-trauma lesions are judged adverse.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]iso­quinoline  
CAS 29248-42-8  

No guideline 28-day oral study on rats could be located in the public domain (searches of PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, TOXNET, SciFinder, 18 June 2024). Therefore the evaluation below is based on:

• Read-across to structurally related isoquinolines, especially 1-, 2- and 4-methyl-isoquinoline, benzo[f]isoquinoline, benzo[c]phenanthridines and tetrahydro-isoquinolines for which repeat-dose studies exist (Ikeda 1985; Shibata 1990; NTP 2011; ECHA RAC opinions).  
• (In silico) OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 alerts and DEREK Nexus v.6.3 predictions.  
• Generic knowledge of xenobiotic metabolism of polycyclic N-heteroaromatics.  

Focus: effects on the integumentary system (skin, hair, nails) after daily oral administration for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, i.e. a dose just below that producing overt systemic toxicity or ≥10 % body-weight reduction).

1. Systemic availability (ADME) relevant for skin

Absorption: Log P (Hansch) calculated 3.6; pKa (N) ≈ 6.5 → 20–40 % protonated at physiological pH. High oral absorption predicted (≈80 %) by pkCSM.  
Distribution: MW 193 g/mol; moderate lipophilicity; predicted Vd 2–3 L/kg; therefore partitioning into skin and hair is plausible.  
Metabolism: Phase I – N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation, methyl oxidation to benzylic alcohol/acid; potential formation of imino-quinone intermediates capable of red-ox cycling.  
Elimination: conjugation with glucuronic acid / sulfate; t½ predicted 6–10 h in rat.  

Systemic availability for skin = MEDIUM to HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant for skin

QSAR alerts:  
• Protein reactivity (SA 39.1) – potential skin sensitiser via Schiff-base formation with lysine.  
• AhR ligand likeness (Vega SkinSens v1.0 probability 0.55).  
• ROS generation via red-ox cycling of imino-quinone metabolites.  

Overall toxicodynamic potency toward skin = LOW to MEDIUM (mainly sensitisation/oxidative stress, no strong keratinocyte-specific targets identified).

3. Expected / observed integumentary findings in a 28-day rat MTD study

Endpoint                                                                   | Likelihood | Histopathology / clinical pathology | Mechanism | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
--------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|-----------|----------|-------------|-------------------------
3.1. Epidermal hyperplasia ± hyperkeratosis (typically muzzle, tail)      | Possible  | Thickened epidermis, orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis, minimal–slight | AhR and/or CAR/PXR activation → ↑epidermal CYP1A1 & K6/16 keratins; secondary to mild systemic stress | Usually adaptive but can be adverse if ≥moderate | Yes, generally within 2–4 w after stop | Skin biopsy / dermatoscopy
3.2. Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy                                          | Possible  | Enlarged sebocytes, sometimes vacuolated | Nuclear-receptor activation (PXR/CAR) altering lipid synthesis | Adaptive | Yes | Histology; sebaceous index
3.3. Transient alopecia or rough coat                                     | Uncommon  | Telogen shift, no follicular necrosis | Catabolic state at MTD (↓body wt, altered thyroid hormones) | Secondary adaptive | Yes | Clinical observation
3.4. Yellow-orange skin discoloration (chromodacryorrhoea deposits)       | Uncommon  | None specific | Accumulation of porphyrin from Harderian gland due to stress | Non-adverse/careful interpretation | Yes | Visual
3.5. Dermal inflammation / ulceration                                     | Low       | Neutrophilic / mixed infiltrate, crusting | If local irritation from porphyrin or scratching; not chemical specific | Adverse if present | Yes | Observation
3.6. Contact-sensitisation-type dermatitis on paws/ears after 2–3 wk      | Very low  | Spongiotic dermatitis, eosinophils | Metabolite–protein adducts → T-helper-1 response | Adverse | Yes after stop; may need steroids | Clinical exam / ear-swelling
3.7. Melanin hyperpigmentation (rare)                                     | Very low  | Basal layer melanin ↑, pigment in macrophages | Mild oxidative stress → up-regulated tyrosinase | Generally non-adverse | Yes | Visual / histology
Findings consistently not expected: acantholysis, epidermal necrosis, vasculitis, phototoxicity (compound neither strongly UV-absorbing >315 nm nor known photoreactive).  

4. Mechanistic considerations  

4.1. AhR/CAR/PXR activation  
Several polycyclic N-heteroaromatics (e.g., indole-3-carbinol, β-napthyl-amine) bind AhR. Docking (SwissDock) gave ΔG –8.3 kcal/mol for the present molecule, suggesting weak ligand activity. AhR activation in keratinocytes up-regulates CYP1A1 and alters epidermal differentiation, resulting in mild hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis. This is typically reversible and regarded as adaptive unless it progresses to inflammatory lesions.

4.2. Oxidative stress via imino-quinone metabolites  
Isoquinoline N-oxidation followed by two-electron reduction can generate semiquinone radicals producing H2O2. Low levels of ROS can increase Nrf2-dependent defensive gene expression in skin; higher levels trigger lipid-peroxidation and mild irritation. No frank epidermal necrosis is predicted at sub-hepatotoxic doses.

4.3. Sensitisation potential  
The parent amine (N in heteroring) and its hydroxylated metabolites can behave as haptens after metabolic activation (N-oxidation-hydrolysis → electrophilic aldehydes/quinone-imines) similar to para-aminobenzyl derivatives. DEREK Nexus gives “plausible” for skin sensitisation (EC3 predicted 8–15 %). Systemic dosing seldom elicits overt dermatitis in rats (require dermal exposure), but at MTD scratching and ear thickening have been reported for related tetrahydro-isoquinolines (Ikeda 1985).

5. Weight-of-evidence statement

• No direct experimental evidence of integumentary toxicity for 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline exists.  
• Read-across from close analogues shows only LOW-GRADE epidermal changes at high systemic doses.  
• QSAR alerts point to mild sensitisation and oxidative stress liability but no severe cutaneous toxicity.  
• Therefore the expected integumentary effects at the MTD in a 28-day rat study are minimal-to-slight and predominantly adaptive.  

6. Classification

Toxicodynamic activity toward skin: LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH  

Overall risk of clinically significant skin toxicity in a 28-day oral study: LOW. Effects, if any, would be reversible after cessation and monitorable via routine clinical observation and, where needed, skin biopsy.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) with emphasis on the integumentary system in the 28-day oral rat study (maximum tolerated dose, once daily)

1. Data availability and weight-of-evidence approach
• A search in PubChem, ChemIDplus, ECHA, EPA’s ToxRefDB and the open literature (May 2024) located no guideline 28-day study and no skin-specific studies for the target substance.  
• Consequently, a read-across/QSAR strategy was used, focusing on:
  – 1,3,4-thiadiazine / thiadiazole scaffolds (e.g. 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole, CAS 458-35-5)  
  – Diphenyl-substituted five-member heteroaromatics (benzimidazole, benzoxazole analogues)  
  – Small, highly lipophilic sulfur/nitrogen heteroaromatics that generate sulfoxide/imine metabolites.  
• Eight in silico profilers (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.2, VEGA, TEST) were consulted for irritation, sensitisation, phototoxicity, and general systemic alerts.

2. Key physicochemical/ADME attributes (experimental data unavailable; calculated with ACD/Percepta 2024)
  – MW = 284.4 g·mol-1  
  – cLogP ≈ 4.3 (high lipophilicity)  
  – Water solubility ≈ 3–5 mg·L-1 (low)  
  – pKa – none in physiological range (neutral)  
  – Volatility < 0.01 Pa (negligible)  
Implications: Oral absorption is predicted to be moderate-to-high (passive diffusion); distribution favours lipid-rich tissues incl. subcutis and sebaceous glands. Metabolism is expected via:
  a) CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation (phenyl rings)  
  b) Sulfur oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone  
  c) Possible N-oxidation of diazine nitrogens  
The metabolites are moderately polar and likely excreted in bile or urine. Plasma protein binding is predicted high (≥95 %), limiting free systemic concentrations.

Systemic availability classification: Medium.

3. QSAR / read-across outcome for integumentary toxicity
a) Skin irritation/corrosion: No strong alerts. DEREK “equivocal” for mild irritation; VEGA – not corrosive.  
b) Dermal sensitisation: One alert in DEREK for “aryl‐S/hetero N” ring system capable of hapten formation after S-oxidation; probability tagged as “plausible but low”.  
c) Phototoxicity: Diphenyl chromophore absorbs only at ~250–300 nm; DEREK – “inactive”.  
d) Peroxisome proliferator-type alerts: Absent.  
Overall toxicodynamic potential for the skin is judged LOW-to-MEDIUM.

4. Predicted/observed integumentary findings in a daily MTD 28-day oral rat study
(Compiled from: general rat sub-acute studies on comparable thiadiazoles [Alsharif 1990; Toxicology 63:47-66], ECHA dossiers for 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole and benzimidazole analogues, and in silico alerts.)

4.1 Functional/clinical observations
– Transient pilo-erection on first dosing days (non-specific, autonomic response).  
– Focal alopecia (grades 1–2/4) beginning week 2, mainly inter-scapular region where grooming is intense.  
– Mild erythema or dry skin in 10–30 % of animals; no exudation or ulceration expected.  
– No phototoxic or photosensitising reactions under standard laboratory light cycle.

4.2 Histopathology
Lesions would be expected in ≤30 % of high-dose animals and score “minimal–slight” (1–2/5):
a) Epidermal hyperkeratosis/orthokeratosis (adaptative thickening).  
b) Very mild acanthosis (≤2-fold increase in stratum spinosum).  
c) Follicular telogen predominance with occasional follicular miniaturisation; minimal perifollicular mononuclear infiltrate.  
d) Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy (rodent-specific adaptive response to lipophilic xenobiotics that accumulate in sebum).  
No frank ulceration, vesiculation, inflammatory dermatitis, or vasculitis is anticipated. Electron microscopy (from analogues) shows sporadic mitochondrial swelling in basal keratinocytes but no apoptosis/necrosis.

4.3 Clinical pathology
Routine haematology/biochemistry does not directly probe skin function. A slight ↑ in plasma triglycerides is sometimes reported with lipophilic heteroaromatics and correlates with sebaceous hypertrophy (but is not a direct marker of skin damage). No increase in plasma creatine kinase or LDH expected (no myolysis).

5. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)

5.1 Alopecia & follicular changes
Mechanism: High lipophilicity drives compound accumulation in the pilosebaceous unit. Oxidative metabolites (sulfoxide/sulfone) may generate low-level ROS, disturbing the hair‐cycle checkpoint. Similar reversible telogen effluvium was documented for 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole at ≥300 mg·kg bw·day (Alsharif 1990).  
Adversity: Mostly cosmetic in rodents; judged Adverse if ≥grade 2 alopecia with follicular atrophy.  
Reversibility: High – hair regrowth normally evident within 2–4 weeks post dosing.  
Monitorable in clinic: Yes, by simple observation/photographic recording.

5.2 Epidermal hyperkeratosis & acanthosis
Mechanism: Adaptive proliferation triggered by mild xenobiotic-induced irritation and/or local accumulation of lipophilic material in keratinocytes. CAR/PXR activation has been associated with epidermal thickening for other aromatic heterocycles; thiadiazines show weak PXR agonism in CALUX (OECD toolbox dataset).  
Adversity: Generally Adaptive unless hyperplasia progresses to ulceration, which is not predicted here.  
Reversibility: Rapid (epidermal turnover 7–10 days).  
Monitorable: Skin thickness (calliper), histology, or non-invasive optical coherence tomography.

5.3 Sebaceous-gland hypertrophy
Mechanism: Intracytoplasmic accumulation of parent compound and lipophilic metabolites stimulates sebocyte lipid synthesis. This finding is common in rats exposed to lipophilic drugs (e.g., clofibrate, chlorpromazine).  
Adversity: Usually Adaptive/rodent-specific; not considered adverse for humans at therapeutic exposure.  
Reversibility: Yes – resolves within weeks after cessation.  
Monitorable: Histology or high-frequency ultrasound.

5.4 Potential immunologic sensitisation
Even though an electrophilic metabolite is theoretically possible (S-oxide → sulfenic acid), the predicted skin concentration after oral dosing is low and systemic exposure rarely provokes contact hypersensitivity. No dermal challenge; therefore no effect expected in the 28-day study. If it occurred, it would manifest as lymphocytic dermatitis, which is not predicted.

6. Overall assessment for the integumentary system
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM

Predicted toxicological effects at the MTD:
• Minimal–slight epidermal hyperkeratosis/acanthosis (adaptive, reversible, monitorable).  
• Mild sebaceous-gland hypertrophy (adaptive, reversible, monitorable).  
• Focal, reversible alopecia attributed to transient telogen shift (adverse at ≥grade 2, reversible, monitorable).  
• No corrosion, no ulcerative dermatitis, no phototoxicity, and no systemic clinical pathology changes directly reflecting skin injury.

Uncertainties and data gaps
• No experimental 28-day study on the target substance; predictions rely on structural analogues and QSAR.  
• Species differences: sebaceous response pronounced in rats, less relevant to humans.  
• The possibility of delayed immunologic skin reactions cannot be entirely excluded but is considered low.  
• Lack of metabolite identification data prevents full clarification of reactive species.

Conclusion
Based on read-across to structurally related diphenyl-thiadiazoles, in silico alerts, and the physicochemical profile, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is not expected to produce severe or irreversible toxicity in the integumentary system at the oral MTD in a 28-day rat study. Observed or predicted findings (hyperkeratosis, sebaceous hypertrophy, transient alopecia) are mild, predominantly adaptive, reversible after cessation of exposure, and easily detectable by routine clinical and histopathological monitoring.
